subject: Chemistry
question_html: Would you expect benzaldehyde to be more reactive or less reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions than propanal? Explain your answer.
question_text: Would you expect benzaldehyde to be more reactive or less reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions than propanal? Explain your answer.
explanation: The carbon atom of the carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic than the carbon atom of the carbonyl group present in propanal. The polarity of the carbonyl group is reduced in benzaldehyde due to resonance making it less reactive in nucleophilic addition reaction as shown below and hence it is less reactive than propanal.
solution: The carbon atom of the carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic than the carbon atom of the carbonyl group present in propanal. The polarity of the carbonyl group is reduced in benzaldehyde due to resonance making it less reactive in nucleophilic addition reaction as shown below and hence it is less reactive than propanal.
solution_html: The carbon atom of the carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic than the carbon atom of the carbonyl group present in propanal. The polarity of the carbonyl group is reduced in benzaldehyde due to resonance making it less reactive in nucleophilic addition reaction as shown below and hence it is less reactive than propanal.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. How can acetaldehyde be prepared from acetyl chloride?
  2. Propanal is more reactive than propanone towards nucleophilic addition reaction. Give reason.
question_text: How can acetaldehyde be prepared from acetyl chloride? Propanal is more reactive than propanone towards nucleophilic addition reaction. Give reason.
explanation: Acetyl chloride is hydrogenated over catalyst, palladium-barium sulphate to prepare acetaldehyde/ Due to less steric hinderance and greater electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanal than propanone./ Due to more steric hinderance and less electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanone than propanal
solution: Acetyl chloride is hydrogenated over catalyst, palladium-barium sulphate to prepare acetaldehyde/ Due to less steric hinderance and greater electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanal than propanone./ Due to more steric hinderance and less electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanone than propanal
solution_html:
1. Acetyl chloride is hydrogenated over catalyst, palladium-barium sulphate to prepare acetaldehyde/
  2. Due to less steric hinderance and greater electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanal than propanone./ Due to more steric hinderance and less electrophilicity of carbonyl carbon in propanone than propanal

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons for the following:

  1. Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. {tex}{HCHO}{/tex} is more reactive than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} towards addition of HCN.
  3. Vinyl group directly attached with carboxylic acid should decrease the acidity of corresponding carboxylic acid due to resonance, but on the contrary it increases the acidity.
question_text: Give reasons for the following: Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. {tex}{HCHO}{/tex} is more reactive than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} towards addition of HCN. Vinyl group directly attached with carboxylic acid should decrease the acidity of corresponding carboxylic acid due to resonance, but on the contrary it increases the acidity.
explanation: Because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride (Lewis acid) gets bonded to the carboxyl group.(forms salt) Because carbonyl carbon of HCHO is more electrophilic than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} / due to +I effect of methyl group/ steric effect of methyl group, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} is less reactive. Because of greater electronegativity of {tex}s p^{2}{/tex} hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached.
solution: Because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride (Lewis acid) gets bonded to the carboxyl group.(forms salt) Because carbonyl carbon of HCHO is more electrophilic than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} / due to +I effect of methyl group/ steric effect of methyl group, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} is less reactive. Because of greater electronegativity of {tex}s p^{2}{/tex} hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached.
solution_html:
1. Because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride (Lewis acid) gets bonded to the carboxyl group.(forms salt)
  2. Because carbonyl carbon of HCHO is more electrophilic than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} / due to +I effect of methyl group/ steric effect of methyl group, {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} is less reactive.
  3. Because of greater electronegativity of {tex}s p^{2}{/tex} hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give plausible explanation for each of the following:

  1. Propanal is more reactive towards addition of HCN than propanone.
  2.  Alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex})-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature.
  3. A base has to be added for an effective reaction of carbonyl compounds with HCN.
question_text: Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Propanal is more reactive towards addition of HCN than propanone. Alpha ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} )-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature. A base has to be added for an effective reaction of carbonyl compounds with HCN.
explanation: Because of more electrophilic carbonyl carbon and less steric hindrance. Because of resonance stabilised conjugate base. Base generates cyanide ion, which being a stronger nucleophile, readily adds to the carbonyl compounds.
solution: Because of more electrophilic carbonyl carbon and less steric hindrance. Because of resonance stabilised conjugate base. Base generates cyanide ion, which being a stronger nucleophile, readily adds to the carbonyl compounds.
solution_html:
1. Because of more electrophilic carbonyl carbon and less steric hindrance.
  2. Because of resonance stabilised conjugate base.
  3. Base generates cyanide ion, which being a stronger nucleophile, readily adds to the carbonyl compounds.

Explain the following reactions and write chemical equation involved:

  1. Wolff-Kishner reduction
  2. Etard reaction
  3. Cannizzaro reaction
question_text: Explain the following reactions and write chemical equation involved: Wolff-Kishner reduction Etard reaction Cannizzaro reaction
explanation: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_{2}{/tex} group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with sodium or potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol Chromyl chloride oxidises methyl group of toluene to a chromium complex, which on hydrolysis gives corresponding benzaldehyde. Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction reaction on heating with concentrated alkali gives salt of carboxylic acid and alcohol
solution: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_{2}{/tex} group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with sodium or potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol Chromyl chloride oxidises methyl group of toluene to a chromium complex, which on hydrolysis gives corresponding benzaldehyde. Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction reaction on heating with concentrated alkali gives salt of carboxylic acid and alcohol
solution_html:
1. The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_{2}{/tex} group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with sodium or potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol
  2. Chromyl chloride oxidises methyl group of toluene to a chromium complex, which on hydrolysis gives corresponding benzaldehyde.
  3. Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction reaction on heating with concentrated alkali gives salt of carboxylic acid and alcohol

question_html:
Give plausible explanation for each of the following:

  1. Sodium hydrogen sulphite is used for separation and purification of aldehydes.
  2. 2, 2, 6-trimethylcyclohexanone does not form cyanohydrin.
  3. Only one {tex}-{NH}_{2}{/tex} group of semicarbazide is involved in the formation of semicarbazone.
question_text: Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Sodium hydrogen sulphite is used for separation and purification of aldehydes. 2, 2, 6-trimethylcyclohexanone does not form cyanohydrin. Only one {tex}-{NH}_{2}{/tex} group of semicarbazide is involved in the formation of semicarbazone.
explanation: Because aldehydes form water soluble hydrogensulphite addition compounds which can be converted back to the aldehydes on treating it with dilute mineral acid or alkali. Due to steric hindrance created by three methyl groups/Due to steric hindrance. The other {tex}-{NH}_{2}{/tex} group is in resonance with the carbonyl group.
solution: Because aldehydes form water soluble hydrogensulphite addition compounds which can be converted back to the aldehydes on treating it with dilute mineral acid or alkali. Due to steric hindrance created by three methyl groups/Due to steric hindrance. The other {tex}-{NH}_{2}{/tex} group is in resonance with the carbonyl group.
solution_html:
1. Because aldehydes form water soluble hydrogensulphite addition compounds which can be converted back to the aldehydes on treating it with dilute mineral acid or alkali.
  2. Due to steric hindrance created by three methyl groups/Due to steric hindrance.
  3. The other {tex}-{NH}_{2}{/tex} group is in resonance with the carbonyl group.

  2. Give a chemical test to distinguish between benzaldehyde and acetophenone.
question_text: Carboxylic acid is more acidic than phenol. Give reason. Give a chemical test to distinguish between benzaldehyde and acetophenone.
explanation: In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalized over two electronegative oxygen atoms, hence carboxylate ion is more stable than phenoxide ion./Due to two equivalent resonating structures of carboxylate ion. Add NaOH and iodine to both the compounds and heat, acetophenone gives yellow ppt of iodoform ( {tex}{CHI}_{3}{/tex} ), whereas Benzaldehyde does not.
solution: In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalized over two electronegative oxygen atoms, hence carboxylate ion is more stable than phenoxide ion./Due to two equivalent resonating structures of carboxylate ion. Add NaOH and iodine to both the compounds and heat, acetophenone gives yellow ppt of iodoform ( {tex}{CHI}_{3}{/tex} ), whereas Benzaldehyde does not.
solution_html:
1. In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalized over two electronegative oxygen atoms, hence carboxylate ion is more stable than phenoxide ion./Due to two equivalent resonating structures of carboxylate ion.
  2. Add NaOH and iodine to both the compounds and heat, acetophenone gives yellow ppt of iodoform ({tex}{CHI}_{3}{/tex}), whereas Benzaldehyde does not.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:

  1. Propanal and propanone.
  2. Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid.
question_text: Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Propanal and propanone. Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid.
explanation: Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NH 3 + 2H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid Both can be distinguished by using sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO 3 ) test. Benzoic acid being an acid reacts with NaHCO 3 solution to produce brisk effervescence due to evolution of CO 2 gas while benzaldehyde does not. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzolc acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Benzaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No (effervescence (due to the evolution of CO 2 gas)
solution: Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NH 3 + 2H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid Both can be distinguished by using sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO 3 ) test. Benzoic acid being an acid reacts with NaHCO 3 solution to produce brisk effervescence due to evolution of CO 2 gas while benzaldehyde does not. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzolc acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Benzaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No (effervescence (due to the evolution of CO 2 gas)
solution_html:
1. Propanal and propanone
    These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 3OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NH3 + 2H2O
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
  2. Benzaldehyde and benzoic acid Both can be distinguished by using sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) test. Benzoic acid being an acid reacts with NaHCO3 solution to produce brisk effervescence due to evolution of CO2 gas while benzaldehyde does not.
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzolc acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Benzaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + NaHCO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No (effervescence (due to the evolution of CO2 gas)

question_text: Account for the following: Oxidation of aldehydes is easier as compared to ketones. The alpha ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) hydrogen atoms of aldehydes are acidic in nature. Write the products in the following reactions: + (CH 3 ) 2 Cd {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} ? + CdCl 2 Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between ethanoic acid and ethanol.
explanation: Because oxidation of aldehyde involves cleavage of C - H bond which is weaker than C - C bond of ketone. Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. On heating with NaOH + l 2 , ethanal gives yellow ppt. Of CHI 3 whereas ethanoic acid does not.
solution: Because oxidation of aldehyde involves cleavage of C - H bond which is weaker than C - C bond of ketone. Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. On heating with NaOH + l 2 , ethanal gives yellow ppt. Of CHI 3 whereas ethanoic acid does not.
solution_html:
1. Because oxidation of aldehyde involves cleavage of C - H bond which is weaker than C - C bond of ketone.
    2. Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base.
  3. On heating with NaOH + l2, ethanal gives yellow ppt. Of CHI3 whereas ethanoic acid does not.

Arrange the following in decreasing order of their acidic strength: CH 3 COOH, O 2 N-CH 2 -COOH, HCOOH Name the reagent in the following reaction: CH 3 -CH = CH-CH 2 -CN {tex}\xrightarrow?{/tex} CH 3 -CH = CH-CH 2 -CHO Write the reaction involved in Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.
explanation: Because one - NH 2 group adjacent to carbonyl group is involved in resonance with it. Steric and electronic reasons / In acetone two alkyl groups reduce the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon more effectively than in acetaldehyde. O 2 N - CH 2 COOH > HCOOH > CH 3 COOH DIBAL H/ H 2 O {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/{\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}}\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm X=\mathrm{Cl},\mathrm{Br}}{\underset{\mathrm X}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}
solution: Because one - NH 2 group adjacent to carbonyl group is involved in resonance with it. Steric and electronic reasons / In acetone two alkyl groups reduce the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon more effectively than in acetaldehyde. O 2 N - CH 2 COOH > HCOOH > CH 3 COOH DIBAL H/ H 2 O {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/{\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}}\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm X=\mathrm{Cl},\mathrm{Br}}{\underset{\mathrm X}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Because one - NH2 group adjacent to carbonyl group is involved in resonance with it.
    2. Steric and electronic reasons / In acetone two alkyl groups reduce the electrophilicity of the
      carbonyl carbon more effectively than in acetaldehyde.
    1. O2N - CH2COOH > HCOOH > CH3COOH
    2. DIBAL H/ H2O
  3. {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/{\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}}\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm X=\mathrm{Cl},\mathrm{Br}}{\underset{\mathrm X}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Account for the following:
    1. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex})-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature.
    2. Oxidation of aldehydes is easier than ketones.
  2. Arrange the following in:
    1. Decreasing reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction propanal, acetone, benzaldehyde.
    2. Increasing order of boiling point:
      Propane, Ethanol, Dimethylether, Propanal
  3. Give simple chemical test to distinguish between Benzoic acid and Benzaldehyde.
question_text: Account for the following: The alpha ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} )-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature. Oxidation of aldehydes is easier than ketones. Arrange the following in: Decreasing reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction propanal, acetone, benzaldehyde. Increasing order of boiling point: Propane, Ethanol, Dimethylether, Propanal Give simple chemical test to distinguish between Benzoic acid and Benzaldehyde.
explanation: Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. Due to cleavage of C-H bond in aldehydes is easier than C-C bond in ketones. propanal > benzaldehyde > Acetone Propane < dimethyl ether < propanal < ethanol Benzoic acid will give brisk effervescence on reacting with sodium bicarbonate whereas benzaldehyde does not.
solution: Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. Due to cleavage of C-H bond in aldehydes is easier than C-C bond in ketones. propanal > benzaldehyde > Acetone Propane < dimethyl ether < propanal < ethanol Benzoic acid will give brisk effervescence on reacting with sodium bicarbonate whereas benzaldehyde does not.
solution_html:
1. Electron withdrawing nature of carbonyl group/ Due to resonance stabilization of the conjugate base.
    2. Due to cleavage of C-H bond in aldehydes is easier than C-C bond in ketones.
    1. propanal > benzaldehyde > Acetone
    2. Propane < dimethyl ether < propanal < ethanol
  3. Benzoic acid will give brisk effervescence on reacting with sodium bicarbonate whereas benzaldehyde does not.

Write the reaction of compound (A) with 2, 4-Dinitrophenyl hydrazine and Fehling solution Write the equation of compound (A) when it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction.
explanation: The compound (A) is an aldehyde. The IUPAC name of the given compound is 2-ethylbenzaldehyde.
solution: The compound (A) is an aldehyde. The IUPAC name of the given compound is 2-ethylbenzaldehyde.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} is more reactive than {tex}{CH}_{3} {COCH}_{3}{/tex} towards reaction with HCN.
  2. Carboxylic acids are higher boiling liquids than aldehydes and ketones.
question_text: Account for the following: {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} is more reactive than {tex}{CH}_{3} {COCH}_{3}{/tex} towards reaction with HCN. Carboxylic acids are higher boiling liquids than aldehydes and ketones.
explanation: In {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} carbonyl carbon is more electrophilic and has less steric hinderance than {tex}{CH}_{3} {COCH}_{3}{/tex} . Due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids/due to dimer formation.
solution: In {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} carbonyl carbon is more electrophilic and has less steric hinderance than {tex}{CH}_{3} {COCH}_{3}{/tex} . Due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids/due to dimer formation.
solution_html:
1. In {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} carbonyl carbon is more electrophilic and has less steric hinderance than {tex}{CH}_{3} {COCH}_{3}{/tex}.
  2. Due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids/due to dimer formation.

question_text: An organic compound (X) having molecular formula C 5 H 10 O can show various properties depending on its structures. Draw each of the structures if it gives positive iodoform test. shows Cannizzaro's reaction. reduces Tollens' reagent and has a chiral carbon. Write the reaction involved in the following: Wolff-Kishner reduction Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
explanation: Give positive iodoform test {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} Show cannizaro reaction (CH 3 ) 3 C - CHO Reduce tollen's reagent {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}-}}\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm X\;=\;\mathrm{Cl},\;\mathrm{Br}}{\mathrm R-\underset{\underset{\mathrm X}\vert}{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}
solution: Give positive iodoform test {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} Show cannizaro reaction (CH 3 ) 3 C - CHO Reduce tollen's reagent {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}-}}\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm X\;=\;\mathrm{Cl},\;\mathrm{Br}}{\mathrm R-\underset{\underset{\mathrm X}\vert}{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Give positive iodoform test{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}
    2. Show cannizaro reaction (CH3)3C - CHO
    3. Reduce tollen's reagent{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{\mathrm H}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}-}}\mathrm{CHO}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\mathrm R-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm X}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm X\;=\;\mathrm{Cl},\;\mathrm{Br}}{\mathrm R-\underset{\underset{\mathrm X}\vert}{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}

question_text: Distinguish with a suitable chemical test: CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 and CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CHO Ethanal and Ethanoic acid Write the structure of oxime of acetone. Identify A to D.
explanation: Add Iodine (I 2 ), NaOH, and heat both the test tubes containing the given organic compounds. Butanone gives yellow precipitate (CHI 3 ) while butanal will not give the positive iodoform test. Add NaHCO 3 in both the test tube containing ethanal and ethanoic acid. Ethanoic acid will give brisk effervescence of CO 2 and ethanal will not. Structure of oxime of acetone is as follows A = CH 3 COCl, B = CH 3 CHO, C = (CH 3 ) 2 CH(OH), D = CH 3 CH 2 OH

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why is the C - O bond length in phenols less than that in methanol?
  2. Arrange the following in order of increasing boiling point:
    Ethoxyethane, Butanal, Butanol, n-butane
  3. How can phenol be prepared from anisole? Give reaction.
question_text: Why is the C - O bond length in phenols less than that in methanol? Arrange the following in order of increasing boiling point: Ethoxyethane, Butanal, Butanol, n-butane How can phenol be prepared from anisole? Give reaction.
explanation: Partial double bond character due to resonance in phenol and no resonance in methanol/sp 2 hybridisation in phenol and sp 3 hybridisation in methanol therefore the C-H bond length in phenol is less than methanol. n-Butane < Ethoxyethane < Butanal < Butanol C 6 H 5 OCH 3 + HX {tex}\to{/tex} C 6 H 5 OH + CH 3 X
solution: Partial double bond character due to resonance in phenol and no resonance in methanol/sp 2 hybridisation in phenol and sp 3 hybridisation in methanol therefore the C-H bond length in phenol is less than methanol. n-Butane < Ethoxyethane < Butanal < Butanol C 6 H 5 OCH 3 + HX {tex}\to{/tex} C 6 H 5 OH + CH 3 X
solution_html:
1. Partial double bond character due to resonance in phenol and no resonance in methanol/sp2 hybridisation in phenol and sp3 hybridisation in methanol therefore the C-H bond length in phenol is less than methanol.
  2. n-Butane < Ethoxyethane < Butanal < Butanol
  3. C6H5OCH3 + HX {tex}\to{/tex} C6H5OH + CH3X

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the reaction involved in Cannizaro’s reaction.
  2. Why are the boiling point of aldehydes and ketones lower than that of corresponding carboxylic acids?
  3. An organic compound A with molecular formula C5H8O2 is reduced to n-pentane with hydrazine followed by heating with NaOH and Glycol. A forms a dioxime with hydroxylamine and gives a positive Iodoform and Tollen’s test. Identify A and give its reaction for Iodoform and Tollen’s test.
question_text: Write the reaction involved in Cannizaro’s reaction. Why are the boiling point of aldehydes and ketones lower than that of corresponding carboxylic acids? An organic compound A with molecular formula C 5 H 8 O 2 is reduced to n-pentane with hydrazine followed by heating with NaOH and Glycol. A forms a dioxime with hydroxylamine and gives a positive Iodoform and Tollen’s test. Identify A and give its reaction for Iodoform and Tollen’s test.
explanation: Aldehydes which do not have an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) reaction on treatment with a concentrated alkali. Reaction involve in cannizaro reaction is as follows Association occurs in carboxylic acids due to the development of intermolecular H-bonding. As a result, the boiling point rises faster than that of comparable molecular mass aldehydes, ketones, and alcohols. {tex}\mathrm A\;=\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} /4-oxopentanal {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{heat}]{\mathrm{NaOH}\;+\;{\mathrm I}_2}\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{warm}]{{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+,\;\mathrm{OH}^-}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COO}^-+\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow{/tex}
solution: Aldehydes which do not have an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) reaction on treatment with a concentrated alkali. Reaction involve in cannizaro reaction is as follows Association occurs in carboxylic acids due to the development of intermolecular H-bonding. As a result, the boiling point rises faster than that of comparable molecular mass aldehydes, ketones, and alcohols. {tex}\mathrm A\;=\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} /4-oxopentanal {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{heat}]{\mathrm{NaOH}\;+\;{\mathrm I}_2}\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{warm}]{{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+,\;\mathrm{OH}^-}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COO}^-+\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Aldehydes which do not have an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atom, undergo self-oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) reaction on treatment with a concentrated alkali. Reaction involve in cannizaro reaction is as follows 
  2. Association occurs in carboxylic acids due to the development of intermolecular H-bonding. As a result, the boiling point rises faster than that of comparable molecular mass aldehydes, ketones, and alcohols.
  3. {tex}\mathrm A\;=\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex}/4-oxopentanal
    {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{heat}]{\mathrm{NaOH}\;+\;{\mathrm I}_2}\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}{/tex}
    {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[\mathrm{warm}]{{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+,\;\mathrm{OH}^-}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\underset{\underset{\mathrm O}{\vert\vert}}{\mathrm C}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{COO}^-+\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give a chemical test to distinguish between ethanal and ethanoic acid.
  2. Why is the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature?
  3. An organic compound A with molecular formula C4H8O2 undergoes acid hydrolysis to form two compounds B and C. Oxidation of C with acidified potassium permanganate also produces B. Sodium salt of B on heating with soda lime gives methane.
    1. Identify A, B and C.
    2. Out of B and C, which will have higher boiling point? Give reason.
question_text: Give a chemical test to distinguish between ethanal and ethanoic acid. Why is the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature? An organic compound A with molecular formula C 4 H 8 O 2 undergoes acid hydrolysis to form two compounds B and C . Oxidation of C with acidified potassium permanganate also produces B . Sodium salt of B on heating with soda lime gives methane. Identify A , B and C . Out of B and C , which will have higher boiling point? Give reason.
explanation: Distinguish ethanal and ethanoic acid - Sodium carbonate test: Ethanal does not react with metal carbonates like sodium carbonates, whereas ethanoic acid does, forming salt, water, and CO 2 . Effervescence will be detected in this reaction. For example: 2CH 3 COOH + Na 2 CO 3 {tex}\to{/tex} 2 CH 3 COONa + CO 2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} + H 2 O Due to strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and the resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base, aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature therefore they are acidic in nature. A = C 4 H 8 O 2 [CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 ] ester Reactions involved are {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm A}{{\mathrm{COOC}}_2{\mathrm H}_5}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\text{ dil }{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{SO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}{/tex} Then {tex}\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}\;\xrightarrow[{\lbrack\mathrm O\rbrack}]{{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}{/tex} So A = CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 , B = CH 3 COOH, C = CH 3 CH 2 OH B has a higher boiling point than C. Because of their propensity to generate intermolecular H-bonds, carboxylic acids have higher boiling temperatures than alcohols.
solution: Distinguish ethanal and ethanoic acid - Sodium carbonate test: Ethanal does not react with metal carbonates like sodium carbonates, whereas ethanoic acid does, forming salt, water, and CO 2 . Effervescence will be detected in this reaction. For example: 2CH 3 COOH + Na 2 CO 3 {tex}\to{/tex} 2 CH 3 COONa + CO 2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} + H 2 O Due to strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and the resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base, aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature therefore they are acidic in nature. A = C 4 H 8 O 2 [CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 ] ester Reactions involved are {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm A}{{\mathrm{COOC}}_2{\mathrm H}_5}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\text{ dil }{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{SO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}{/tex} Then {tex}\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}\;\xrightarrow[{\lbrack\mathrm O\rbrack}]{{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}{/tex} So A = CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 , B = CH 3 COOH, C = CH 3 CH 2 OH B has a higher boiling point than C. Because of their propensity to generate intermolecular H-bonds, carboxylic acids have higher boiling temperatures than alcohols.
solution_html:
1. Distinguish ethanal and ethanoic acid - Sodium carbonate test: Ethanal does not react with metal carbonates like sodium carbonates, whereas ethanoic acid does, forming salt, water, and CO2. Effervescence will be detected in this reaction.
    For example: 2CH3COOH + Na2CO3 {tex}\to{/tex} 2 CH3COONa + CO2 {tex}\uparrow{/tex} + H2O
  2. Due to strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and the resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base, aldehydes and ketones are acidic in nature therefore they are acidic in nature.
    1. A = C4H8O2 [CH3COOC2H5] ester Reactions involved are
      {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm A}{{\mathrm{COOC}}_2{\mathrm H}_5}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{\text{ dil }{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{SO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}{/tex}
      Then
      {tex}\underset{\mathrm C}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}}\;\xrightarrow[{\lbrack\mathrm O\rbrack}]{{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}\underset{\mathrm B}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}}{/tex}
      So A = CH3COOC2H5, B = CH3COOH, C = CH3CH2OH
    2. B has a higher boiling point than C. Because of their propensity to generate intermolecular H-bonds, carboxylic acids have higher boiling temperatures than alcohols.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The carbon – oxygen double bond is polarised in aldehydes and ketones due to higher electronegativity of oxygen relative to carbon. Therefore they undergo nucleophilic addition reactions with a number of nucleophiles such as HCN, NaHSO3, alcohols, ammonia derivatives and Grignard reagents. Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents as compared to ketones. The carbonyl group of carboxylic acid does not give reactions of aldehydes and ketones. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.

  2. Write a chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone.
question_text: The carbon – oxygen double bond is polarised in aldehydes and ketones due to higher electronegativity of oxygen relative to carbon. Therefore they undergo nucleophilic addition reactions with a number of nucleophiles such as HCN, NaHSO 3 , alcohols, ammonia derivatives and Grignard reagents. Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents as compared to ketones. The carbonyl group of carboxylic acid does not give reactions of aldehydes and ketones. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols. Propanone gives yellow precipitate (CHI 3 ) while propanal will not give yellow precipitate.

Propanone gives yellow precipitate (CHI 3 ) while propanal will not give yellow precipitate.

  2. (1) Add Iodine (I2), NaOH, and heat both the test tubes containing the given organic compounds. Propanone gives yellow precipitate (CHI3) while propanal will not give yellow precipitate.

Identify A, B, C and D and write all the reactions involved.
Write one chemical test to distinguish between compound B and propanone.
question_text: Compound A undergoes Rosenmund reduction to give compound B with molecular formula C 7 H 6 O. Compound B does not give Fehling's test but reacts with conc. NaOH to give C and D. Identify A, B, C and D and write all the reactions involved. Write one chemical test to distinguish between compound B and propanone.
explanation: A = Benzoyl chloride / C 6 H 5 COCl B = Benzaldehyde / C 6 H 5 CHO C = Benzyl alcohol / C 6 H 5 CH 2 OH D = Sodium Benzoate / C 6 H 5 COONa Benzoyl chloride Benzaldehyde Iodoform test: Add Iodine (I 2 ) and NaOH in both the test tubes containing the given organic compounds and heat. Propanone gives +ve iodoform test i.e., CHI 3 (yellow ppt.) while benzaldehyde does not.
solution: A = Benzoyl chloride / C 6 H 5 COCl B = Benzaldehyde / C 6 H 5 CHO C = Benzyl alcohol / C 6 H 5 CH 2 OH D = Sodium Benzoate / C 6 H 5 COONa Benzoyl chloride Benzaldehyde Iodoform test: Add Iodine (I 2 ) and NaOH in both the test tubes containing the given organic compounds and heat. Propanone gives +ve iodoform test i.e., CHI 3 (yellow ppt.) while benzaldehyde does not.
solution_html:
A = Benzoyl chloride / C6H5COCl
B = Benzaldehyde / C6H5CHO
C = Benzyl alcohol / C6H5CH2OH
D = Sodium Benzoate / C6H5COONa

question_html: Compound A with molecular formula (C2H6O) on oxidation by PCC gives compound B, which on treatment with dilute alkali forms compound C which is a {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydroxy aldehyde. B on oxidation by potassium permanganate forms C. Identify A, B, C and D and write all the chemical equations involved.
question_text: Compound A with molecular formula (C 2 H 6 O) on oxidation by PCC gives compound B, which on treatment with dilute alkali forms compound C which is a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehyde. B on oxidation by potassium permanganate forms C. Identify A, B, C and D and write all the chemical equations involved.
explanation: A = Ethanol / CH 3 CH 2 OH B = Ethanal / CH 3 CHO C = 3-Hydroxybutanal / CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CHO D = Ethanoic acid / CH 3 COOH The chemical equations involved are as following {tex}CH_3CH_2OH\;\xrightarrow{PCC}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex} {tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex} {tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{OH}\vert}{CH}-CH_2-CHO{/tex}
solution: A = Ethanol / CH 3 CH 2 OH B = Ethanal / CH 3 CHO C = 3-Hydroxybutanal / CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CHO D = Ethanoic acid / CH 3 COOH The chemical equations involved are as following {tex}CH_3CH_2OH\;\xrightarrow{PCC}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex} {tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex} {tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{OH}\vert}{CH}-CH_2-CHO{/tex}
solution_html:
A = Ethanol / CH3CH2OH
B = Ethanal / CH3CHO
C = 3-Hydroxybutanal / CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO
D = Ethanoic acid / CH3COOH
The chemical equations involved are as following
{tex}CH_3CH_2OH\;\xrightarrow{PCC}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex}
{tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{KMnO_4}CH_3COOH{/tex}
{tex}CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{OH}\vert}{CH}-CH_2-CHO{/tex}

question_text: Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their acidic strength: {tex}Cl - CH_2 - COOH, Cl - \underset {\underset {Cl}|}CH -COOH, CCl_3 - COOH{/tex} Write the IUPAC name of the given compound: CH 3 COCH 2 COCH 3

solution: Greater the number of electron withdrawing substituents, greater would be the dispersal of the negative charge and hence stronger will be the acid. {tex}Cl - CH_2 - COOH < Cl - \underset {\underset {Cl}|}CH -COOH < CCl_3 - COOH{/tex} ​​ The IUPAC name of the compound is: Pentan-2,4-dione
solution_html:

    {tex}Cl - CH_2 - COOH < Cl - \underset {\underset {Cl}|}CH -COOH < CCl_3 - COOH{/tex}​​

question_text: Write the equations involved in the following reactions: Cannizzaro reaction Aldol condensation Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
explanation: Cannizzaro reaction {tex}2R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H\xrightarrow{NaOH}{/tex} {tex}R-CH_2OH+R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-ONa{/tex} Aldol condensation {tex}\underbrace{CH_3-\underset{\underset H\vert}{\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C}+{\overset{\overset H\vert}{CH}}_2-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}{\underset{\underset H\vert}C}}_{2\;molecules\;of\;acetaldehyde}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{Acetaldol}{\underset H\vert}}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}C}-CH_{{}_2}-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H{/tex} Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
solution: Cannizzaro reaction {tex}2R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H\xrightarrow{NaOH}{/tex} {tex}R-CH_2OH+R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-ONa{/tex} Aldol condensation {tex}\underbrace{CH_3-\underset{\underset H\vert}{\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C}+{\overset{\overset H\vert}{CH}}_2-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}{\underset{\underset H\vert}C}}_{2\;molecules\;of\;acetaldehyde}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{Acetaldol}{\underset H\vert}}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}C}-CH_{{}_2}-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H{/tex} Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
solution_html:
1. Cannizzaro reaction
    {tex}2R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H\xrightarrow{NaOH}{/tex} {tex}R-CH_2OH+R-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-ONa{/tex}
  2. Aldol condensation
    {tex}\underbrace{CH_3-\underset{\underset H\vert}{\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C}+{\overset{\overset H\vert}{CH}}_2-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}{\underset{\underset H\vert}C}}_{2\;molecules\;of\;acetaldehyde}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{Dil.\;NaOH}CH_3-\underset{\underset{Acetaldol}{\underset H\vert}}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}C}-CH_{{}_2}-\overset{\overset O{\vert\vert}}C-H{/tex}
  3. Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction

question_text: How will you carry out the following conversions: (Any two ) Propanal to Propane Ethanal to But-2-enal Ethanoic acid to ethanamide
explanation: {tex}CH_3-\underset{Propan\;ol}{CH_2-CH_2}-OH{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{170^oC}]{H_2SO_4(cons.)}CH_3-CH\;=\;CH_2{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}CH_3-\underset{Propan-2-ol}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{CH}}-CH_3{/tex} {tex}\underset{Ethanal}{CH_3CHO}+dil.NaOH\rightarrow CH_3CH(OH)-CH_2CHO{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{heating}CH3CH=\underset{But-2-enal\;}{CHCHO}{/tex} {tex}\underset{Ethanoic\;acid}{CH_3COOH}\xrightarrow[{Heat}]{NH_3}\underset{Ethanamide}{CH_3CONH_2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}CH_3-\underset{Propan\;ol}{CH_2-CH_2}-OH{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{170^oC}]{H_2SO_4(cons.)}CH_3-CH\;=\;CH_2{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}CH_3-\underset{Propan-2-ol}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{CH}}-CH_3{/tex} {tex}\underset{Ethanal}{CH_3CHO}+dil.NaOH\rightarrow CH_3CH(OH)-CH_2CHO{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{heating}CH3CH=\underset{But-2-enal\;}{CHCHO}{/tex} {tex}\underset{Ethanoic\;acid}{CH_3COOH}\xrightarrow[{Heat}]{NH_3}\underset{Ethanamide}{CH_3CONH_2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}CH_3-\underset{Propan\;ol}{CH_2-CH_2}-OH{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{170^oC}]{H_2SO_4(cons.)}CH_3-CH\;=\;CH_2{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}CH_3-\underset{Propan-2-ol}{\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{CH}}-CH_3{/tex}
  2. {tex}\underset{Ethanal}{CH_3CHO}+dil.NaOH\rightarrow CH_3CH(OH)-CH_2CHO{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{heating}CH3CH=\underset{But-2-enal\;}{CHCHO}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\underset{Ethanoic\;acid}{CH_3COOH}\xrightarrow[{Heat}]{NH_3}\underset{Ethanamide}{CH_3CONH_2}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
What happens when

  1. Propanone is treated with CH3MgBr and then hydrolysed?
  2. Ethanal is treated with excess ethanol and acid?
  3. Methanal undergoes Cannizzaro reaction?
question_text: What happens when Propanone is treated with CH 3 MgBr and then hydrolysed? Ethanal is treated with excess ethanol and acid? Methanal undergoes Cannizzaro reaction?
explanation: When propanone is treated with methylmagnesium Bromide and then hydrolysed, tertiary butyl alcohol is formed. CH 3 - CO - CH 3 + CH 3 MgBr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 C - OMgBr {tex}\xrightarrow{H_3O^+}{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 COH When ethanal reacts with excess ethanol and acid gives acetal. Methanal will undergo cannizzaro reaction as it possesses {tex}\alpha{/tex} - hydrogen to form methanol
solution: When propanone is treated with methylmagnesium Bromide and then hydrolysed, tertiary butyl alcohol is formed. CH 3 - CO - CH 3 + CH 3 MgBr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 C - OMgBr {tex}\xrightarrow{H_3O^+}{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 COH When ethanal reacts with excess ethanol and acid gives acetal. Methanal will undergo cannizzaro reaction as it possesses {tex}\alpha{/tex} - hydrogen to form methanol
solution_html:
1. When propanone is treated with methylmagnesium Bromide and then hydrolysed, tertiary butyl alcohol is formed.
    CH3 - CO - CH3 + CH3MgBr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} (CH3)3C - OMgBr {tex}\xrightarrow{H_3O^+}{/tex} (CH3)3COH
  2. When ethanal reacts with excess ethanol and acid gives acetal.
  3. Methanal will undergo cannizzaro reaction as it possesses {tex}\alpha{/tex} - hydrogen to form methanol

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A compound A (C2H4O) on oxidation gives B (C2H4O2). A undergoes Iodoform reaction to give yellow precipitate and reacts with HCN to form the compound C. C on hydrolysis gives 2-hydroxypropanoic acid. Identify the compounds A, B and C. Write down equations for the reactions involved.
question_text: A compound A (C 2 H 4 O) on oxidation gives B (C 2 H 4 O 2 ). A undergoes Iodoform reaction to give yellow precipitate and reacts with HCN to form the compound C . C on hydrolysis gives 2-hydroxypropanoic acid. Identify the compounds A , B and C . Write down equations for the reactions involved.
explanation: A = Acetaldehyde (CH 3 CHO) B = Ethanoic acid (CH 3 COOH) C = Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (CH 3 CH(OH)CN) {tex}\underset{\mathit(\mathit A\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit H\mathit O}\mathit\;\overset{\mathit\lbrack\mathit O\mathit\rbrack}{\mathit\rightarrow}\mathit\;\underset{\mathit(\mathit B\mathit)}{\mathit C{\mathit H}_{\mathit3}\mathit C\mathit O\mathit O\mathit H}{/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm I}_2\;\mathrm{NAOH}}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3}}{/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm A)}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}}\;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{HCN}}\;\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}​​​​​​​ {/tex} {tex}\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\underset{\mathrm{CN}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}\;\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O/\mathrm H^+}\;\underset{}{\underset{\mathrm{COOH}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OH}}}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Explain the following reactions:

  1. Clemmensen reduction
  2. Stephen reaction
question_text: Explain the following reactions: Clemmensen reduction Stephen reaction
explanation: Clemmensen reduction Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones), that are not sensitive to acids, are heated with zinc amalgam and conc HCl to give alkanes. Carbonyl group is reduced to methylene group. This reaction is often used to convert acyl benzenes to alkyl benzene. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3+4[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}/\;conc.\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex} Stephen reaction Nitriles react with stannous chloride / HCl to form imine hydrochlorides. This is reduction reaction. The imine hydrochlirdes on acid hydrolysis gives aldehydes. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}+2[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{SnCl}}_2/\mathrm{dilHC}1}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NHHCl}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}
solution: Clemmensen reduction Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones), that are not sensitive to acids, are heated with zinc amalgam and conc HCl to give alkanes. Carbonyl group is reduced to methylene group. This reaction is often used to convert acyl benzenes to alkyl benzene. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3+4[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}/\;conc.\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex} Stephen reaction Nitriles react with stannous chloride / HCl to form imine hydrochlorides. This is reduction reaction. The imine hydrochlirdes on acid hydrolysis gives aldehydes. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}+2[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{SnCl}}_2/\mathrm{dilHC}1}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NHHCl}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Clemmensen reduction
    Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones), that are not sensitive to acids, are heated with zinc amalgam and conc HCl to give alkanes. Carbonyl group is reduced to methylene group. This reaction is often used to convert acyl benzenes to alkyl benzene.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3+4[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}/\;conc.\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}
  2. Stephen reaction
    Nitriles react with stannous chloride / HCl to form imine hydrochlorides. This is reduction reaction. The imine hydrochlirdes on acid hydrolysis gives aldehydes.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}+2[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{SnCl}}_2/\mathrm{dilHC}1}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NHHCl}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}

question_text: Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone. Why? How can you distinguish between Acetophenone and Benzophenone? Draw the structure of the following derivative: 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazone of Propanone.
explanation: Because of oxidation of propanal involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of propanone. On heating with NaOH and I 2 , acetophenone gives yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas benzophenone does not.
solution: Because of oxidation of propanal involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of propanone. On heating with NaOH and I 2 , acetophenone gives yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas benzophenone does not.
solution_html:
1. Because of oxidation of propanal involves cleavage of C-H bond which is weaker than C-C bond of propanone.
  2. On heating with NaOH and I2, acetophenone gives yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas benzophenone does not.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids are some of the important classes of organic compounds containing carbonyl group. These are highly polar molecules due to higher electro-negativity of oxygen relative to carbon in the carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by dehydrogenation or controlled oxidation of primary alcohols and controlled reduction of acyl halides. Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation.
Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols.


Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.:
CH3COCH3, CH3CHO, HCHO, C6H5COCH3

Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes. Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction.: CH 3 COCH 3 , CH 3 CHO, HCHO, C 6 H 5 COCH 3
explanation: C 6 H 5 COCH 3 < CH 3 COCH 3 < CH 3 CHO < HCHO
solution: C 6 H 5 COCH 3 < CH 3 COCH 3 < CH 3 CHO < HCHO

Ketones are prepared by oxidation of secondary alcohols and hydration of alkynes. Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions onto the carbonyl group but carboxylic acid does not undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. The alpha ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) - hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones are acidic. Therefore aldehydes and ketones having at least one {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen undergo Aldol condensation. Aldehydes are easily oxidised by mild oxidising agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent. Carboxylic acids are prepared by the oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes and by hydrolysis of nitriles. Aromatic carboxylic acids are prepared by side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzenes. Carboxylic acids are considerably more acidic than alcohols and most of simple phenols. Why carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions like aldehydes and ketones?
explanation: Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.
solution: Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.
solution_html: Because of resonance by -OH of COOH which reduces the electrophilicity of carboxyl carbon / because of resonance in COOH group due to which carbon loses its carbonyl nature.

question_html:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow: (1+1+1+2=5)
A class of organic molecules which contain a carbon atom connected to an oxygen atom by a double bond is called Aldehydes and Ketones. It is called as carbonyl group. Aldehydes are prepared by the oxidation of alcohols. Formaldehyde is sold in an aqueous solution called formalin. Propanone, a simplest ketone is commercially prepared by fermenting corn or by oxidation of propan-2-ol. Carboxylic acids also have carbonyl carbon. They can be prepared by the oxidation of alcohols and aldehydes.
Formic acid was first isolated by the distillation of red ants. It is partially responsible for the pain and irritation of ant and wasp stings. Aldehydes undergo many nucleophilic addition reactions. They can be reduced to primary alcohols. The aldehydes with -hydrogen undergo aldol condensation and the aldehydes without -hydrogen undergo Cannizzaro reaction.
Ketones are highly reactive, although less so than aldehydes. Ketones are obtained by oxidation of secondary alcohols. Ketones possessing α-hydrogens also undergo aldol condensation. Carboxylic acids occur widely in nature and are used in the production of plastics, esters, etc. Aspirin is prepared from acetic acid. Similar to aldehydes and ketones, carboxylic acids can be halogenated at the α-carbon by reacting with a halogen in presence of phosphorus.

  1. Which of the following compounds would undergo aldol condensation? (1)
    Methanal, Benzaldehyde, Ethanal
  2. Write the chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone. (1)
  3. Write the reagent required in the following reaction: (1) 
    CH2 = CH - CH2OH {tex}\xrightarrow {?}{/tex} CH2 = CH - CHO

      OR

    2. Write the chemical reactions for the following: (2)
      1. Clemmensen reduction
      2. HVZ reaction

explanation: Benzaldehyde undergoes aldol condensation in an alkaline medium. The reaction with sodium hypoiodite is the distinguishing test between propanal and propanone. This test is known as the iodoform test. Pyridinium chlorochromate (C 5 H 5 NHCrO 3 Cl) or Cu at 573 K are the reagents required. {tex}(\mathrm X)-{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{OH}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{{\mathrm K}_2{\mathrm{Cr}}_2{\mathrm O}_7}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3(\mathrm y){/tex} (X) with molecular formula C 3 H 8 O is an alcohol. CH 3 -CH 2- CH 2 -OH, propanol. But since K 2 Cr 2 O 7 being a strong oxidising agent would oxidise 1 o alcohol to carboxylic acid, which will give negative iodoform test, thus (X) has to be a 2 o alcohol. Acetone would give positive iodoform test. OR (I) (II)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give the mechanism for the formation of ethanol from ethene.
  2. Predict the reagent for carrying out the following conversions:
    1. Phenol to benzoquinone
    2. Anisole to p-bromoanisole
    3. Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol
question_text: Give the mechanism for the formation of ethanol from ethene. Predict the reagent for carrying out the following conversions: Phenol to benzoquinone Anisole to p-bromoanisole Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol
explanation: Three steps are involved in the mechanism of hydration of ethene to yield ethanol. (1) The electrophillic attack of hydronium ion protonates ethene to form carbocation. (2) Water (as a nucleophile) attacks carbocation. (3) Deprotonation gives ethanol. The mechanism is shown above. Reagent for carrying out the following conversions : Phenol to benzoquinone K 2 Cr 2 O 7 + H 2 SO 4 Anisole to p-bromoanisole Br2 in CH 3 COOH Br2 aq. / Bromine water Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol
solution: Three steps are involved in the mechanism of hydration of ethene to yield ethanol. (1) The electrophillic attack of hydronium ion protonates ethene to form carbocation. (2) Water (as a nucleophile) attacks carbocation. (3) Deprotonation gives ethanol. The mechanism is shown above. Reagent for carrying out the following conversions : Phenol to benzoquinone K 2 Cr 2 O 7 + H 2 SO 4 Anisole to p-bromoanisole Br2 in CH 3 COOH Br2 aq. / Bromine water Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol
solution_html:
1. Three steps are involved in the mechanism of hydration of ethene to yield ethanol.
    (1) The electrophillic attack of hydronium ion protonates ethene to form carbocation.
    (2) Water (as a nucleophile) attacks carbocation.
    (3) Deprotonation gives ethanol.
    The mechanism is shown above.
  2. Reagent for carrying out the following conversions :
    1. Phenol to benzoquinone
      K2Cr2O7 + H2SO4
    2. Anisole to p-bromoanisole
      Br2 in CH3COOH
    3. Br2 aq. / Bromine water
      Phenol to 2,4,6-tribromophenol

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. An organic compound (A) having molecular formula C4H8O gives orange red precipitate with 2, 4-DNP reagent. It does not reduce Tollens’ reagent but gives yellow precipitate of iodoform on heating with NaOH and I2. Compound (A) on reduction with NaBH4 gives compound (B) which undergoes dehydration reaction on heating with conc. H2SO4 to form compound (C). Compound (C) on Ozonolysis gives two molecules of ethanal.
    Identify (A), (B) and (C) and write their structures. Write the reactions of compound (A) with (i) NaOH/I2 and (ii) NaBH4.
  2. Give reasons:
    1. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone.
    2. {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones is acidic in nature.
question_text: An organic compound (A) having molecular formula C 4 H 8 O gives orange red precipitate with 2, 4-DNP reagent. It does not reduce Tollens’ reagent but gives yellow precipitate of iodoform on heating with NaOH and I 2 . Compound (A) on reduction with NaBH 4 gives compound (B) which undergoes dehydration reaction on heating with conc. H 2 SO 4 to form compound (C). Compound (C) on Ozonolysis gives two molecules of ethanal. Identify (A), (B) and (C) and write their structures. Write the reactions of compound (A) with (i) NaOH/I 2 and (ii) NaBH 4 . Give reasons: Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone. {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones is acidic in nature.
explanation: The molecular formula of compound (A) is C 4 H 8 O, which suggests that it may be an aldehyde or a ketone. The positive reaction with 2,4-DNP also supports this idea. The negative Tollens' test indicates that it is not an aldehyde. The positive reaction with NaOH and I 2 suggests that it has a methyl ketone group (CH 3 CO-). Therefore, compound (A) is 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone) with the structure CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 . When 2-butanone (A) is reduced with NaBH 4 , it forms the corresponding alcohol, which is compound (B). The reaction is: CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + NaBH 4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 Compound (B) is 3-pentanol with the structure CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 . When 3-pentanol (B) is dehydrated with conc. H 2 SO 4 , it forms compound (c), which is an alkene. The reaction is: CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 + H 2 O When 2-pentene (C) is subjected to ozonolysis, it forms two molecules of ethanal. The reaction is: CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 + O 3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CHO + CH 3 CHO Therefore, the structures of compounds (A), (B), and (C) are: (A) 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone): CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 (B) 3-pentanol: CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 (C) 2-pentene: CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 Reactions of compound (A): (i) Reaction with NaOH/I 2 : CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + 4NaOH + 2I 2 → 2NaI + CHI 3 + CH 3 COONa + 3H 2 O The yellow precipitate formed in this reaction is due to the formation of iodoform (CHI 3 ). (ii) Reaction with NaBH 4 : CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + NaBH 4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 The product formed in this reaction is compound (B), 3-pentanol. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone because oxidation of aldehyde is simpler than ketones as in propanal there is one oxidisable-H existing attached to the carbonyl carbon whereas propanone existing ketone does not have the oxidisable-H atom. Due to the acidic nature of α-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones, they undergo a number of reactions. This acidity is because of the strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base.
solution: The molecular formula of compound (A) is C 4 H 8 O, which suggests that it may be an aldehyde or a ketone. The positive reaction with 2,4-DNP also supports this idea. The negative Tollens' test indicates that it is not an aldehyde. The positive reaction with NaOH and I 2 suggests that it has a methyl ketone group (CH 3 CO-). Therefore, compound (A) is 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone) with the structure CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 . When 2-butanone (A) is reduced with NaBH 4 , it forms the corresponding alcohol, which is compound (B). The reaction is: CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + NaBH 4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 Compound (B) is 3-pentanol with the structure CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 . When 3-pentanol (B) is dehydrated with conc. H 2 SO 4 , it forms compound (c), which is an alkene. The reaction is: CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 + H 2 O When 2-pentene (C) is subjected to ozonolysis, it forms two molecules of ethanal. The reaction is: CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 + O 3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CHO + CH 3 CHO Therefore, the structures of compounds (A), (B), and (C) are: (A) 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone): CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 (B) 3-pentanol: CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 (C) 2-pentene: CH 3 CH=CHCH 3 Reactions of compound (A): (i) Reaction with NaOH/I 2 : CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + 4NaOH + 2I 2 → 2NaI + CHI 3 + CH 3 COONa + 3H 2 O The yellow precipitate formed in this reaction is due to the formation of iodoform (CHI 3 ). (ii) Reaction with NaBH 4 : CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + NaBH 4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH 3 CH(OH)CH 2 CH 3 The product formed in this reaction is compound (B), 3-pentanol. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone because oxidation of aldehyde is simpler than ketones as in propanal there is one oxidisable-H existing attached to the carbonyl carbon whereas propanone existing ketone does not have the oxidisable-H atom. Due to the acidic nature of α-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones, they undergo a number of reactions. This acidity is because of the strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base.
solution_html:
1. The molecular formula of compound (A) is C4H8O, which suggests that it may be an aldehyde or a ketone. The positive reaction with 2,4-DNP also supports this idea. The negative Tollens' test indicates that it is not an aldehyde. The positive reaction with NaOH and I2 suggests that it has a methyl ketone group (CH3CO-).
    Therefore, compound (A) is 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone) with the structure CH3COCH2CH3.
    When 2-butanone (A) is reduced with NaBH4, it forms the corresponding alcohol, which is compound (B). The reaction is:
    CH3COCH2CH3 + NaBH4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3
    Compound (B) is 3-pentanol with the structure CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3.
    When 3-pentanol (B) is dehydrated with conc. H2SO4, it forms compound (c), which is an alkene. The reaction is:
    CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH3CH=CHCH3 + H2O
    When 2-pentene (C) is subjected to ozonolysis, it forms two molecules of ethanal. The reaction is:
    CH3CH=CHCH3 + O3 {tex}\to{/tex} CH3CHO + CH3CHO
    Therefore, the structures of compounds (A), (B), and (C) are:
    (A) 2-butanone (methyl ethyl ketone): CH3COCH2CH3
    (B) 3-pentanol: CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3
    (C) 2-pentene: CH3CH=CHCH3
    Reactions of compound (A):
    (i) Reaction with NaOH/I2:
    CH3COCH2CH3 + 4NaOH + 2I2 → 2NaI + CHI3 + CH3COONa + 3H2O
    The yellow precipitate formed in this reaction is due to the formation of iodoform (CHI3).
    (ii) Reaction with NaBH4:
    CH3COCH2CH3 + NaBH4 {tex}\to{/tex} CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3
    The product formed in this reaction is compound (B), 3-pentanol.
    1. Oxidation of propanal is easier than propanone because oxidation of aldehyde is simpler than ketones as in propanal there is one oxidisable-H existing attached to the carbonyl carbon whereas propanone existing ketone does not have the oxidisable-H atom.
    2. Due to the acidic nature of α-hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones, they undergo a number of reactions. This acidity is because of the strong electron-withdrawing nature of the carbonyl groups and resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base.

solution: Cyanohydrin of cyclobutanone Hemiacetal of ethanol {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{OH}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{CrO}}_3}\lbrack{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{\mathrm O}\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}-\mathrm H\rbrack{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\lbrack\mathrm H^+\rbrack}\lbrack{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{\mathrm O}\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}-\mathrm{OH}\rbrack{/tex} By iodoform test: Propanone on treatment with I 2 /NaOH undergoes iodoform test to give a yellow ppt. of iodoform. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COCH}}_3+3\mathrm{NaOI}\rightarrow\underset{\mathrm{Yellow}\;\mathrm{ppt}.}{{\mathrm{CHI}}_3\downarrow}{/tex} {tex}+{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\;+2\mathrm{NaOH}{/tex} Propanal does not give this test.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Give reasons:
    1. Benzoic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid.
    2. Methanal is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction than ethanal.
  2. Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone.
question_text: Give reasons: Benzoic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid. Methanal is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction than ethanal. Give a simple chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone.
explanation: Due to greater electronegativity of sp 2 hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached and greater resonance stabilization of carboxylate ion with the benzene ring, benzoic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid. Methanal is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction than ethanal because carbonyl carbon of methanal is more electrophilic than that of ethanol. Also, due to +I effect of methyl group in ethanal, reactivity of ethanal decreases. Chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone: Tollen’s test is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones. Aldehydes gets easily oxidized, wheareas ketones they are not readily oxidized. Reaction: Propanal forms silver mirror whereas propanone does not form.
solution: Due to greater electronegativity of sp 2 hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached and greater resonance stabilization of carboxylate ion with the benzene ring, benzoic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid. Methanal is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction than ethanal because carbonyl carbon of methanal is more electrophilic than that of ethanol. Also, due to +I effect of methyl group in ethanal, reactivity of ethanal decreases. Chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone: Tollen’s test is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones. Aldehydes gets easily oxidized, wheareas ketones they are not readily oxidized. Reaction: Propanal forms silver mirror whereas propanone does not form.
solution_html:
1. Due to greater electronegativity of sp2 hybridised carbon to which carboxyl carbon is attached and greater resonance stabilization of carboxylate ion with the benzene ring, benzoic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid.
    2. Methanal is more reactive towards nucleophilic addition reaction than ethanal because carbonyl carbon of methanal is more electrophilic than that of ethanol. Also, due to +I effect of methyl group in ethanal, reactivity of ethanal decreases.
  2. Chemical test to distinguish between propanal and propanone:
    Tollen’s test is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones.
    Aldehydes gets easily oxidized, wheareas ketones they are not readily oxidized.
    Reaction:

    Propanal forms silver mirror whereas propanone does not form.

question_text: Butan-1-ol has a higher boiling point than diethyl ether. Why? Write the mechanism of the following reaction: 2CH 3 CH 2 OH {tex}\xrightarrow[413 \mathrm{~K}]{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 - O - CH 2 - CH 3
explanation: Both are functional isomers. However, intermolecular hydrogen bonding is present in butan-1-ol molecules while it is absent in the molecules of diethyl ether .Therefore, boiling point of alcohol (390 K) is higher as compared to that of ether (308 K)

solution: The carboxyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to CH 2 group on treatment with zinc-amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid (Clemmensen reduction) {tex}\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{HCl}]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}} \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}
solution_html:
The carboxyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to CH2 group on treatment with zinc-amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid (Clemmensen reduction)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
What happens when

  1. Sodium phenoxide is treated with CH3Cl?
  2. CH2 = CH - CH2 - OH is oxidised by PCC?
  3. Phenol is treated with CH3COCl/anhydrous AlCl3?

Write chemical equations in support of your answer.
question_text: What happens when Sodium phenoxide is treated with CH 3 Cl? CH 2 = CH - CH 2 - OH is oxidised by PCC? Phenol is treated with CH 3 COCl/anhydrous AlCl 3 ? Write chemical equations in support of your answer.
explanation: This reaction is known as Williamson synthesis Here, Sodium alkoxide reacts with primary alkyl halide to give corresponding ether. Propenal is formed CH 3 = CH-CH 2 -OH {tex}\xrightarrow{\text { PCC }}{/tex} CH 3 = CH-CH PCC (Pyridinium chlorochromate) oxidises primary alchols to aldehydes. when substituted benzene is treated with acid chloride in presence of anhydrous aluminum chloride, it affords the corresponding ketone. This reaction is known as Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.
solution: This reaction is known as Williamson synthesis Here, Sodium alkoxide reacts with primary alkyl halide to give corresponding ether. Propenal is formed CH 3 = CH-CH 2 -OH {tex}\xrightarrow{\text { PCC }}{/tex} CH 3 = CH-CH PCC (Pyridinium chlorochromate) oxidises primary alchols to aldehydes. when substituted benzene is treated with acid chloride in presence of anhydrous aluminum chloride, it affords the corresponding ketone. This reaction is known as Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.
solution_html:
1. This reaction is known as Williamson synthesis Here, Sodium alkoxide reacts with primary alkyl halide to give corresponding ether.
  2. Propenal is formed
    CH3 = CH-CH2-OH {tex}\xrightarrow{\text { PCC }}{/tex} CH3 = CH-CH
    PCC (Pyridinium chlorochromate) oxidises primary alchols to aldehydes.
  3. when substituted benzene is treated with acid chloride in presence of anhydrous aluminum chloride, it affords the corresponding ketone. This reaction is known as Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.

question_html:
1. Give reasons:
    1. HCHO is more reactive than CH3 - CHO towards addition of HCN.
    2. pKa of O2N-CH2-COOH is lower than that of CH3-COOH.
    3. Alpha hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones is acidic in nature.
  2. Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:
    1. Ethanal and Propanal
    2. Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one
question_text: Give reasons: HCHO is more reactive than CH 3 - CHO towards addition of HCN. pK a of O 2 N-CH 2 -COOH is lower than that of CH 3 -COOH. Alpha hydrogen of aldehydes and ketones is acidic in nature. Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Ethanal and Propanal Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one
explanation: Due to +I effect of methyl group in CH 3 CHO. Due to -I effect of nitro group in nitroacetic acid. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. Add NaOH and I 2 to both the compounds and heat, ethanal gives yellow ppt of iodoform. Add NaOH and I 2 to both the compounds and heat, pentan-2-one gives yellow ppt of iodoform.
solution: Due to +I effect of methyl group in CH 3 CHO. Due to -I effect of nitro group in nitroacetic acid. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilization of the conjugate base. Add NaOH and I 2 to both the compounds and heat, ethanal gives yellow ppt of iodoform. Add NaOH and I 2 to both the compounds and heat, pentan-2-one gives yellow ppt of iodoform.
solution_html:
1. Due to +I effect of methyl group in CH3CHO.
    2. Due to -I effect of nitro group in nitroacetic acid.
    3. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilization of the conjugate base.
    1. Add NaOH and I2 to both the compounds and heat, ethanal gives yellow ppt of iodoform.
    2. Add NaOH and I2 to both the compounds and heat, pentan-2-one gives yellow ppt of iodoform.
