explanation: Cyclohexanone when reacts with hydrogen cyanide (HCN) it form cyclohexanone cyanohydrin The sodium benzoate reacts with soda lime to give benzene But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex} Butanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shiny silver mirror but butan-2-one being a ketone does not reduces Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{Butanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,\,\,reagent} + 3\mathop O\limits^ - H\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CO{O^ - } + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {Silver}\limits_{mirror} } + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } C{H_3}}\limits_{Butan - 2 - one} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron\,\,phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Voilet\,\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex} But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } Fe + 3HCl{/tex}
solution: Cyclohexanone when reacts with hydrogen cyanide (HCN) it form cyclohexanone cyanohydrin The sodium benzoate reacts with soda lime to give benzene But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex} Butanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shiny silver mirror but butan-2-one being a ketone does not reduces Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{Butanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,\,\,reagent} + 3\mathop O\limits^ - H\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CO{O^ - } + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {Silver}\limits_{mirror} } + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } C{H_3}}\limits_{Butan - 2 - one} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron\,\,phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Voilet\,\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex} But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } Fe + 3HCl{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Cyclohexanone when reacts with hydrogen cyanide (HCN) it form cyclohexanone cyanohydrin

    2. The sodium benzoate reacts with soda lime to give benzene
    3. But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give 
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex}
    1. Butanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shiny silver mirror but butan-2-one being a ketone does not reduces Tollen's reagent.
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{Butanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,\,\,reagent} + 3\mathop O\limits^ - H\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CO{O^ - } + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {Silver}\limits_{mirror} } + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } C{H_3}}\limits_{Butan - 2 - one} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
    2. Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration.
      {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron\,\,phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Voilet\,\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex}
      But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate.
      {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } Fe + 3HCl{/tex}

question_html:
1. Write the products of the following reaction
    2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow[{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}]{\mathrm{Dry}\;\mathrm{ether}}{/tex}
    3. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{COOH}+{\mathrm{Br}}_2\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{Phosphorus}}{/tex}
  2. Write simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds?
    1. Propanal and propanone
    2. Benzaldehyde and Benzoic acid
question_text: Write the products of the following reaction {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{MgBr}+{\mathrm{CO}}_2\xrightarrow[{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}]{\mathrm{Dry}\;\mathrm{ether}}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{COOH}+{\mathrm{Br}}_2\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{Phosphorus}}{/tex} Write simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds? Propanal and propanone Benzaldehyde and Benzoic acid
explanation: CH 3 -COOH CH 3 -CH(Br)-COOH Add ammonical solution of silver nitrate/Tollen's reagent to both the compounds, propanal will give silver mirror while propanone does not. Add NaHCO 3 solution to both the compounds, benzoic acid will give effervescence and liberate CO 2 while benzaldehyde will not.
solution: CH 3 -COOH CH 3 -CH(Br)-COOH Add ammonical solution of silver nitrate/Tollen's reagent to both the compounds, propanal will give silver mirror while propanone does not. Add NaHCO 3 solution to both the compounds, benzoic acid will give effervescence and liberate CO 2 while benzaldehyde will not.
solution_html:
2. CH3-COOH
    3. CH3-CH(Br)-COOH
    1. Add ammonical solution of silver nitrate/Tollen's reagent to both the compounds, propanal will give silver mirror while propanone does not.
    2. Add NaHCO3 solution to both the compounds, benzoic acid will give effervescence and liberate CO2 while benzaldehyde will not.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write products in the following:
    1. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} CHO}\xrightarrow{{Dil\,\,NaOH}}{/tex}
    2.  {tex}\xrightarrow[{Conc\,\,HCl}]{{Zn - Hg}}{/tex}
  2. Give simple test to distinguish the following pairs of compounds:
    1. Ethanal and propanal
    2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone
    3. Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate
question_text: Write products in the following: {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} CHO}\xrightarrow{{Dil\,\,NaOH}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Conc\,\,HCl}]{{Zn - Hg}}{/tex} Give simple test to distinguish the following pairs of compounds: Ethanal and propanal Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate
explanation: {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{Ethanol} \xrightarrow[{cross\,\,aldol\,\,condensation}]{{Dil\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{3 - hydroxy\,\,butanal}{/tex} Ethanal and propanal: Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate of CHI 3 . While propanal does not give this test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H}\limits_{Ethanal} + 3NaOI\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + 2NaOH + CH{I_3} \downarrow (yellow\,ppt){/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{Propanal} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt of CHI 3 Benzaldehyde and acetophenone: Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO 3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to {C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{Ethanol} \xrightarrow[{cross\,\,aldol\,\,condensation}]{{Dil\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{3 - hydroxy\,\,butanal}{/tex} Ethanal and propanal: Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate of CHI 3 . While propanal does not give this test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H}\limits_{Ethanal} + 3NaOI\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + 2NaOH + CH{I_3} \downarrow (yellow\,ppt){/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{Propanal} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt of CHI 3 Benzaldehyde and acetophenone: Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO 3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to {C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{Ethanol} \xrightarrow[{cross\,\,aldol\,\,condensation}]{{Dil\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{3 - hydroxy\,\,butanal}{/tex}
    1. Ethanal and propanal: Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex}  group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate of CHI3. While propanal does not give this test.
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H}\limits_{Ethanal} + 3NaOI\xrightarrow{\Delta }{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + 2NaOH + CH{I_3} \downarrow (yellow\,ppt){/tex}
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{Propanal} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt of CHI3 
    2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone: Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent.
        {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex}
       {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
    3. Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not.
      {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to {C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Complete the following chenical equations:
    2. CH3COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[]{LiAlH_4}{/tex} 
    3. CH3COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[(b)H_2O]{(a)Cl_2/P}{/tex}
  2. Distinguish between
    1. CH3COOH and 
    2. CH3-CHO and {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - O{/tex}
question_text: Complete the following chenical equations: CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[]{LiAlH_4}{/tex} CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[(b)H_2O]{(a)Cl_2/P}{/tex} Distinguish between CH 3 COOH and CH 3 -CHO and {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - O{/tex}
explanation: CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[]{LiAlH_4}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OH CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[(b)H_2O]{(a)Cl_2/P}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{Cl} {H_2} - COOH}\limits_{\alpha - chlorocarboxylic{\text{ }}acid} {/tex} a. Phenol and acetic acid: When both are reacted with NaHCO 3 , acetic acid gives brisk effervescence due to the liberation of CO 2 gas. But phenol does not give any reaction with NaHCO 3 . CH 3 COOH + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \, + \,C{O_{2\,}} \uparrow \, + \,{H_2}O{/tex} C 6 H 5 COOH + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No evolution of CO 2 gas. b. Acetaldehyde and acetone: When both are reacted with Tollen's reagent, aldehyde gives silver mirror while acetone does not. The reactions involved are as follows. CH 3 CHO + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + \,H_4{/tex} + 2Ag {tex}\downarrow{/tex} +H 2 O + 3NH 3 {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution: CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[]{LiAlH_4}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OH CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[(b)H_2O]{(a)Cl_2/P}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{Cl} {H_2} - COOH}\limits_{\alpha - chlorocarboxylic{\text{ }}acid} {/tex} a. Phenol and acetic acid: When both are reacted with NaHCO 3 , acetic acid gives brisk effervescence due to the liberation of CO 2 gas. But phenol does not give any reaction with NaHCO 3 . CH 3 COOH + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \, + \,C{O_{2\,}} \uparrow \, + \,{H_2}O{/tex} C 6 H 5 COOH + NaHCO 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No evolution of CO 2 gas. b. Acetaldehyde and acetone: When both are reacted with Tollen's reagent, aldehyde gives silver mirror while acetone does not. The reactions involved are as follows. CH 3 CHO + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + \,H_4{/tex} + 2Ag {tex}\downarrow{/tex} +H 2 O + 3NH 3 {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution_html:
1.  
    2. CH3COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[]{LiAlH_4}{/tex}  CH3CH2OH
    3. CH3COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[(b)H_2O]{(a)Cl_2/P}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{Cl} {H_2} - COOH}\limits_{\alpha - chlorocarboxylic{\text{ }}acid} {/tex}
  2. a. Phenol and acetic acid: When both are reacted with NaHCO3, acetic acid gives brisk effervescence due to the liberation of CO2 gas. But phenol does not give any reaction with NaHCO3.
    CH3COOH + NaHCO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \, + \,C{O_{2\,}} \uparrow \, + \,{H_2}O{/tex}
    C6H5COOH + NaHCO3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No evolution of CO2 gas.

    b. Acetaldehyde and acetone: When both are reacted with Tollen's reagent, aldehyde gives silver mirror while acetone does not. The reactions involved are as follows.
    CH3CHO + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + \,H_4{/tex} + 2Ag{tex}\downarrow{/tex} +H2O + 3NH3 
    {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex}+ {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the structures of A and B in the following reactions:
    1. CH3COCl {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2},Pd-BaS{O_4}}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}N - OH}}{/tex} B
    2. CH3MgBr {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_3}{O^ + }}]{{(i)\,C{O_2}}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}{/tex} B
  2. Distinguish between
    1. C6H5-COCH3 and C6H5-CHO
    2. CH3-COOH and HCOOH
  3. Arrange the following in increasing order of their boiling points:
    CH3CHO, CH3COOH, CH3CH2OH
question_text: Write the structures of A and B in the following reactions: CH 3 COCl {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2},Pd-BaS{O_4}}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}N - OH}}{/tex} B CH 3 MgBr {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_3}{O^ + }}]{{(i)\,C{O_2}}}{/tex} A {tex}\xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}{/tex} B Distinguish between C 6 H 5 -COCH 3 and C 6 H 5 -CHO CH 3 -COOH and HCOOH Arrange the following in increasing order of their boiling points: CH 3 CHO, CH 3 COOH, CH 3 CH 2 OH
explanation: {tex}C{H_3}MgBr\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_3}{O^ + }}]{{(i)\,C{O_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl}\limits_{(B)}{/tex} C 6 H 5 COCH 3 give positive iodoform test whereas C 6 H 5 CHO does not. + NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction HCOOH decolourises pink colour of KMnO 4 (acidifier) whereas CH 3 COOH does not show this test. HCOOH + 2KMnO 4 + 3H 2 SO 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K 2 SO 4 + 5CO 2 + 8H 2 O + MnSO 4 CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}/{H^ + }}}{/tex} no discharge of pink colour of KMnO 4 solution. As carboxylic acid have strongest hydrogen bonding therefore, they have highest boiling points. Next, stronger hydrogen bonding is shown by alcohol. Hence, order of boiling point is as follows: CH 3 CHO < CH 3 CH 2 OH < CH 3 COOH
solution: {tex}C{H_3}MgBr\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_3}{O^ + }}]{{(i)\,C{O_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl}\limits_{(B)}{/tex} C 6 H 5 COCH 3 give positive iodoform test whereas C 6 H 5 CHO does not. + NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction HCOOH decolourises pink colour of KMnO 4 (acidifier) whereas CH 3 COOH does not show this test. HCOOH + 2KMnO 4 + 3H 2 SO 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K 2 SO 4 + 5CO 2 + 8H 2 O + MnSO 4 CH 3 COOH {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}/{H^ + }}}{/tex} no discharge of pink colour of KMnO 4 solution. As carboxylic acid have strongest hydrogen bonding therefore, they have highest boiling points. Next, stronger hydrogen bonding is shown by alcohol. Hence, order of boiling point is as follows: CH 3 CHO < CH 3 CH 2 OH < CH 3 COOH
solution_html:
2. {tex}C{H_3}MgBr\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_3}{O^ + }}]{{(i)\,C{O_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl}\limits_{(B)}{/tex}
    1. C6H5COCH3 give positive iodoform test whereas C6H5CHO does not.

       + NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
    2. HCOOH decolourises pink colour of KMnO4 (acidifier) whereas CH3COOH does not show this test.
      HCOOH + 2KMnO4 + 3H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K2SO4 + 5CO2 + 8H2O + MnSO4 
      CH3COOH {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}/{H^ + }}}{/tex} no discharge of pink colour of KMnO4 solution.
  3. As carboxylic acid have strongest hydrogen bonding therefore, they have highest boiling points. Next, stronger hydrogen bonding is shown by alcohol. Hence, order of boiling point is as follows:
    CH3CHO < CH3CH2OH < CH3COOH

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. What is meant by the following terms? Give example of the reaction in each case.
    1. Aldol
    2. Semicarbazone
  2. Complete the following:
    1. CH3COCl {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2},Pd - BaS{O_4}}}{/tex} 
    2. CH3-CH=CH-CN {tex}\xrightarrow{{DIBALH}}{/tex}
question_text: What is meant by the following terms? Give example of the reaction in each case. Aldol Semicarbazone Complete the following: CH 3 COCl {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2},Pd - BaS{O_4}}}{/tex} CH 3 -CH=CH-CN {tex}\xrightarrow{{DIBALH}}{/tex}
explanation: Aldol: It is a hydroxy ketone or aldehyde. It is formed as a product during aldol condensation reaction. e.g. {tex}2C{H_3} - CHO\xrightarrow{{Dil.\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{\mathop {3 - hydroxybutanal}\limits_{(Aldol)} }{/tex} Semicarbazone: It is a derivative of imines formed by a condensation reaction between a ketone or aldehyde and semicarbazide. e.g. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \xrightarrow[{Rosenmund\,\,reaction}]{{Pd,\,BaS{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} + HCl{/tex} But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CN}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex}
solution: Aldol: It is a hydroxy ketone or aldehyde. It is formed as a product during aldol condensation reaction. e.g. {tex}2C{H_3} - CHO\xrightarrow{{Dil.\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{\mathop {3 - hydroxybutanal}\limits_{(Aldol)} }{/tex} Semicarbazone: It is a derivative of imines formed by a condensation reaction between a ketone or aldehyde and semicarbazide. e.g. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \xrightarrow[{Rosenmund\,\,reaction}]{{Pd,\,BaS{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} + HCl{/tex} But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CN}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Aldol: It is a hydroxy ketone or aldehyde. It is formed as a product during aldol condensation reaction. e.g.
       {tex}2C{H_3} - CHO\xrightarrow{{Dil.\,\,NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{\mathop {3 - hydroxybutanal}\limits_{(Aldol)} }{/tex}
    2. Semicarbazone: It is a derivative of imines formed by a condensation reaction between a ketone or aldehyde and semicarbazide. e.g.
    1. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \xrightarrow[{Rosenmund\,\,reaction}]{{Pd,\,BaS{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} + HCl{/tex}
    2. But-2-en-1 nitrile on reaction with DIBAL-H followed by water give
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CN}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - nitrile} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,DIBAL - H}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - en - 1 - ol}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the chemical reaction involved in Wolff-Kishner reduction.
  2. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction:
    C6H5-COCH3, CH3-CHO, CH3COCH3
  3. Why carboxylic acid does not give reactions of carbonyl group?
  4. Write the product in the following reaction:
    CH3CH2CH=CH-CH2CN {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,{{(t - but)}_2} - AlH}}{/tex}  
  5. A and B are two functional isomers of compound C3H6O. On heating with NaOH and I2, isomer B forms yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas isomer A does not form any precipitate. Write the formula of A and B.
question_text: Write the chemical reaction involved in Wolff-Kishner reduction. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction: C 6 H 5 -COCH 3 , CH 3 -CHO, CH 3 COCH 3 Why carboxylic acid does not give reactions of carbonyl group? Write the product in the following reaction: CH 3 CH 2 CH=CH-CH 2 CN {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,{{(t - but)}_2} - AlH}}{/tex} A and B are two functional isomers of compound C 3 H 6 O. On heating with NaOH and I 2 , isomer B forms yellow precipitate of iodoform whereas isomer A does not form any precipitate. Write the formula of A and B.
explanation: Wolff-Kishner reduction: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH 2 group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with KOH in high boiling solvent like ethylene glycol. This reaction is known as Wolff-Kishner reduction. The increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction is The carboxylic acids may be regarded as resonance hybrid of structures I and II as Similarly, carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones may be regarded as a resonance hybrid of structures III and IV. Due to contribution of structure IV, the carbonyl carbon in aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic. However, due to contribution of structure II of carboxylic acid, the electrophilic character of carboxyl carbon is reduced, hence carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions of carbonyl group. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,{{(t - but)}_2}AlH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Isomer B on heating with NaOH and I 2 form yellow ppt. of iodoform. Hence, contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group having three carbons in total. Hence, Isomer B is {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} whereas isomer A is {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} (a functional isomer of B). {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3} + NaOH + {I_2} \to C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + \mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{(Yellow\,\,ppt)}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO + NaOH + {I_2} \to {/tex} No reaction.
solution: Wolff-Kishner reduction: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH 2 group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with KOH in high boiling solvent like ethylene glycol. This reaction is known as Wolff-Kishner reduction. The increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction is The carboxylic acids may be regarded as resonance hybrid of structures I and II as Similarly, carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones may be regarded as a resonance hybrid of structures III and IV. Due to contribution of structure IV, the carbonyl carbon in aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic. However, due to contribution of structure II of carboxylic acid, the electrophilic character of carboxyl carbon is reduced, hence carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions of carbonyl group. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,{{(t - but)}_2}AlH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Isomer B on heating with NaOH and I 2 form yellow ppt. of iodoform. Hence, contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group having three carbons in total. Hence, Isomer B is {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} whereas isomer A is {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} (a functional isomer of B). {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3} + NaOH + {I_2} \to C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + \mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{(Yellow\,\,ppt)}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO + NaOH + {I_2} \to {/tex} No reaction.
solution_html:
1. Wolff-Kishner reduction: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH2 group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with KOH in high boiling solvent like ethylene glycol. This reaction is known as Wolff-Kishner reduction.
  2. The increasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition reaction is 
  3. The carboxylic acids may be regarded as resonance hybrid of structures I and II as

    Similarly, carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones may be regarded as a resonance hybrid of structures III and IV.

    Due to contribution of structure IV, the carbonyl carbon in aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic. However, due to contribution of structure II of carboxylic acid, the electrophilic character of carboxyl carbon is reduced, hence carboxylic acid does not give nucleophilic addition reactions of carbonyl group.
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,{{(t - but)}_2}AlH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CH = CH - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex}
  5. Isomer B on heating with NaOH and I2 form yellow ppt. of iodoform. Hence, contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group having three carbons in total.
    Hence, Isomer B is {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}{/tex} whereas isomer A is {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} (a functional isomer of B).
    {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3} + NaOH + {I_2} \to C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + \mathop {CH{I_3}}\limits_{(Yellow\,\,ppt)}{/tex}
    {tex}C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO + NaOH + {I_2} \to {/tex} No reaction.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the chemical equations for the reaction involved in cannizzaro reaction.
  2. Draw the structure of semicarbazone of ethanol.
  3. Why p{tex}k_{\alpha}{/tex} of F-CH2 -COOH is lower than that Cl-CH2-COOH?
  4. Write the product in the following reaction
    {tex}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}{/tex}
  5. How can you distinguish between propanal and propanone?
question_text: Write the chemical equations for the reaction involved in cannizzaro reaction. Draw the structure of semicarbazone of ethanol. Why p {tex}k_{\alpha}{/tex} of F-CH 2 -COOH is lower than that Cl-CH 2 -COOH? Write the product in the following reaction {tex}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}{/tex} How can you distinguish between propanal and propanone?
explanation: Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to salt of carboxylic acid. As inductive effect (-I) for F is stronger than of Cl, F.CH 2 COO- ion is more stable than of Cl.CH 2 COO- ion. Hence, F.CH 2 COOH is stronger acid than of F.CH 2 COOH is lower than of Cl.CH 2 COOH. ( {tex}\because{/tex} P {tex}k_{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{acid \ strength}{/tex} ) {tex}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CHO{/tex} Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,reagent} + 3O{H^ - } \to {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{\Pr opanoate\,\,ion} + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{Silver\,mirror} + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Propanone} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
solution: Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to salt of carboxylic acid. As inductive effect (-I) for F is stronger than of Cl, F.CH 2 COO- ion is more stable than of Cl.CH 2 COO- ion. Hence, F.CH 2 COOH is stronger acid than of F.CH 2 COOH is lower than of Cl.CH 2 COOH. ( {tex}\because{/tex} P {tex}k_{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{acid \ strength}{/tex} ) {tex}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CHO{/tex} Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,reagent} + 3O{H^ - } \to {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{\Pr opanoate\,\,ion} + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{Silver\,mirror} + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Propanone} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
solution_html:
1. Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex}-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to salt of carboxylic acid.

  3. As inductive effect (-I) for F is stronger than of Cl, F.CH2COO- ion is more stable than of Cl.CH2COO- ion. Hence, F.CH2COOH is stronger acid than of F.CH2COOH is lower than of Cl.CH2COOH.
    ({tex}\because{/tex} P{tex}k_{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{acid \ strength}{/tex} )
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CN\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O}]{{(i)\,\,DIBAL - H}}C{H_3} - CH = CH - C{H_2}CHO{/tex}
  5. Propanal and propanone
    These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} + \mathop {2{{[Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}]}^ + }}\limits_{Tollen's\,reagent} + 3O{H^ - } \to {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}CO{O^ - }}\limits_{\Pr opanoate\,\,ion} + \mathop {2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{Silver\,mirror} + 4N{H_3} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Propanone} \xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror

question_text: Complete the following equations: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COCH}}_3\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAlH}}_4}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{COOH}\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm b)\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm a)\;{\mathrm{Cl}}_2/\mathrm P}{/tex} Distinguish between: CH 3 COOH and CH 3 -CHO and {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C-{\mathrm{CH}}_3}}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COCH}}_3+2\lbrack\mathrm H\rbrack\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAIH}}_4}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}){\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{COOH}+\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm{Cl}}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm{Cl}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex} When CH 3 COOH is added to an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate, brisk effervescence of CO 2 is evolved. {tex}\underset{\mathrm{Carboylic}\;\mathrm{acid}}{\mathrm{RCOOH}}+{\mathrm{NaHCO}}_3\rightarrow\underset{\mathrm{Sodium}\;\mathrm{carboxylate}}{\mathrm{RCOONa}}\;+\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O+{\mathrm{CO}}_2{/tex} Phenol does not give this test. When CH 3 CHO is heated with Tollen's reagent, they form silver mirror while CH 3 COCH 3 does not. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}+\underset{\mathrm{Tollen}'\mathrm s\;\mathrm{reagent}}{2{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+}+3\mathrm{OH}^-{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\;\mathrm{COO}^-\;\underset{(\mathrm{Silver}\;\mathrm{mirror})}{+2\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow+\;2}{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\;+\;4{\mathrm{NH}}_3{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COCH}}_3+2\lbrack\mathrm H\rbrack\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAIH}}_4}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}){\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{COOH}+\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm{Cl}}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm{Cl}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex} When CH 3 COOH is added to an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate, brisk effervescence of CO 2 is evolved. {tex}\underset{\mathrm{Carboylic}\;\mathrm{acid}}{\mathrm{RCOOH}}+{\mathrm{NaHCO}}_3\rightarrow\underset{\mathrm{Sodium}\;\mathrm{carboxylate}}{\mathrm{RCOONa}}\;+\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O+{\mathrm{CO}}_2{/tex} Phenol does not give this test. When CH 3 CHO is heated with Tollen's reagent, they form silver mirror while CH 3 COCH 3 does not. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}+\underset{\mathrm{Tollen}'\mathrm s\;\mathrm{reagent}}{2{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+}+3\mathrm{OH}^-{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\;\mathrm{COO}^-\;\underset{(\mathrm{Silver}\;\mathrm{mirror})}{+2\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow+\;2}{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\;+\;4{\mathrm{NH}}_3{/tex}
solution_html:
2. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COCH}}_3+2\lbrack\mathrm H\rbrack\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{LiAIH}}_4}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}){\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}
    3. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{COOH}+\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm{ii})\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{(\mathrm i)\;{\mathrm{Cl}}_2/\mathrm{Red}\;\mathrm{phosphorus}}\underset{\mathrm{Cl}}{\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex}
    1. When CH3COOH is added to an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate, brisk effervescence of CO2 is evolved.
      {tex}\underset{\mathrm{Carboylic}\;\mathrm{acid}}{\mathrm{RCOOH}}+{\mathrm{NaHCO}}_3\rightarrow\underset{\mathrm{Sodium}\;\mathrm{carboxylate}}{\mathrm{RCOONa}}\;+\;{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O+{\mathrm{CO}}_2{/tex}
      Phenol does not give this test.
    2. When CH3CHO is heated with Tollen's reagent, they form silver mirror while CH3COCH3 does not.
      {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}+\underset{\mathrm{Tollen}'\mathrm s\;\mathrm{reagent}}{2{\lbrack\mathrm{Ag}{({\mathrm{NH}}_3)}_2\rbrack}^+}+3\mathrm{OH}^-{/tex}{tex}\rightarrow\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\;\mathrm{COO}^-\;\underset{(\mathrm{Silver}\;\mathrm{mirror})}{+2\mathrm{Ag}\downarrow+\;2}{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\;+\;4{\mathrm{NH}}_3{/tex}

question_text: Write the reagents required in the following reactions : CH 2 =CH-CH 2 OH {tex}\stackrel{?}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} CH 2 =CH-CHO CH 3 -COOH {tex}\stackrel{?}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} CH 3 -CONH 2 .
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{CH_2Cl_2}]{{PCC}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} Here, PCC is Pyridinium chlorochromate, a 1 : 2 complex of chromium trioxide pyridine (CrO 3 ·2C 5 H 5 N) which only oxidises OH group and not the = bond. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-H_2O}]{{NH_3/heat}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CONH}_{2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{CH_2Cl_2}]{{PCC}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} Here, PCC is Pyridinium chlorochromate, a 1 : 2 complex of chromium trioxide pyridine (CrO 3 ·2C 5 H 5 N) which only oxidises OH group and not the = bond. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{COOH}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-H_2O}]{{NH_3/heat}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CONH}_{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{CH_2Cl_2}]{{PCC}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} 
    Here, PCC is Pyridinium chlorochromate, a 1 : 2 complex of chromium trioxide pyridine (CrO3 ·2C5H5N) which only oxidises OH group and not the = bond.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Describe the following reactions:
    1. Acetylation
    2. Aldol condensation
  2. Write the main product in the following equations:
    1. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}?{/tex}
    2.  
    3. CH3-COOH {tex}\stackrel{\mathrm{PCl}_{5}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} ?
question_text: Describe the following reactions: Acetylation Aldol condensation Write the main product in the following equations: {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}?{/tex} CH 3 -COOH {tex}\stackrel{\mathrm{PCl}_{5}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} ?
explanation: Acetylation: The introduction of acetyl group (-COCH 3 ) into a chemical compound like phenols, alcohols is called acetylation. Aldol condensation: In this reaction two molecules of an aldehyde or ketone condense in presence of dilute alkali (dil NaOH, Ba(OH) 2 etc.) to form a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehyde or a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxyketone respectively. These {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehydes or ketones are collectively called aldols and the reaction is called aldol condensation. The reaction is based on acidity of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones. For eg: {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - Cl + POC{l_3} + HCl{/tex}
solution: Acetylation: The introduction of acetyl group (-COCH 3 ) into a chemical compound like phenols, alcohols is called acetylation. Aldol condensation: In this reaction two molecules of an aldehyde or ketone condense in presence of dilute alkali (dil NaOH, Ba(OH) 2 etc.) to form a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehyde or a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxyketone respectively. These {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehydes or ketones are collectively called aldols and the reaction is called aldol condensation. The reaction is based on acidity of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones. For eg: {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - Cl + POC{l_3} + HCl{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Acetylation: The introduction of acetyl group (-COCH3) into a chemical compound like phenols, alcohols is called acetylation.
       
    2. Aldol condensation:  In this reaction two molecules of an aldehyde or ketone condense in presence of dilute alkali (dil NaOH, Ba(OH)2 etc.) to form a {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydroxy aldehyde or a {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydroxyketone respectively. These {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydroxy aldehydes or ketones are collectively called aldols and the reaction is called aldol condensation. The reaction is based on acidity of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones. For eg:
       
    1. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H - C{H_3}{/tex}
    3. {tex}C{H_3}COOH\xrightarrow{{PC{l_5}}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - Cl + POC{l_3} + HCl{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Draw the structures of the following:
    1. p-methylbenzaldehyde
    2. 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one
  2. Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:
    1. Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate
    2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone
    3. Phenol and benzoic acid
question_text: Draw the structures of the following: p-methylbenzaldehyde 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Phenol and benzoic acid
explanation: p-methylbenzaldehyde 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one {tex}\mathop C\limits_1 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_2 }\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop C\limits_3 {H_2} = \mathop {\mathop C\limits_4 }\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - \mathop C\limits_5 {H_3}{/tex} Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate: Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO 3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex} Benzaldehyde and acetophenone: Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains -CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Phenol and benzoic acid Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron - phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Violet\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex} But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}Fe}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } + 3HCl{/tex}
solution: p-methylbenzaldehyde 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one {tex}\mathop C\limits_1 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_2 }\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop C\limits_3 {H_2} = \mathop {\mathop C\limits_4 }\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - \mathop C\limits_5 {H_3}{/tex} Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate: Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO 3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not. {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex} Benzaldehyde and acetophenone: Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains -CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Phenol and benzoic acid Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron - phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Violet\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex} But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}Fe}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } + 3HCl{/tex}
solution_html:
1.  
    1. p-methylbenzaldehyde
    2. 4-methylpent-3-en-2-one
      {tex}\mathop C\limits_1 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_2 }\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop C\limits_3 {H_2} = \mathop {\mathop C\limits_4 }\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } - \mathop C\limits_5 {H_3}{/tex}
    1. Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate:
      Benzoic acid produces brisk effervescence with NaHCO3 solution while ethyl benzoate does not.
      {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COOH + NaHC{O_3} \to{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a + {H_2}O + C{O_2} \uparrow{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5}COO{C_2}{H_5} + NaHC{O_3} \to No\,reaction{/tex}
    2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone:
      Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains -CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent.
         {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex}
       {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
    3. Phenol and benzoic acid
      Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration.
      {tex}\mathop {6{C_6}{H_5}OH}\limits_{Phenol} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{[Fe{{(O{C_6}{H_5})}_6}]}^{3 - }}}\limits_{\mathop {Iron - phenol\,\,complex}\limits_{(Violet\,colour)} } + 6{H^ + } + 3C{l^ - }{/tex}
      But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate.
      {tex}\mathop {3{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,\,acid} + FeC{l_3} \to \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_3}Fe}\limits_{\mathop {Ferric\,\,benzoate}\limits_{(Buff\,\,coloured\,ppt)} } + 3HCl{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Draw the structures of the following derivatives:
    1. Propanoneoxime
    2. Semicarbazone of CH3CHO
  2. How will you convert ethanal into the following compounds? Give the chemical equations involved:
    1. CH3 - CH3
    2. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex}
    3. CH3CH2OH
question_text: Draw the structures of the following derivatives: Propanoneoxime Semicarbazone of CH 3 CHO How will you convert ethanal into the following compounds? Give the chemical equations involved: CH 3 - CH 3 {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OH
explanation: Propanone oxime the structure of propanone oxime is {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} = N - OH{/tex} Semicarbazone of CH 3 CHO The structure of semicarbazone of CH 3 CHO is {tex}C{H_3} - CH = N - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}{/tex} . Ethanal (CH 3 CHO) to CH 3 -CH 3 CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} HCl \\ Clemmensen \\ reduction \end{subarray} ]{{Zn - Hg}}{/tex} CH 3 -CH 3 + H 2 O Ethanal, (CH 3 CHO) to {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} 2CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} Aldol \\ condensation \end{subarray} ]{{Dil\;NaOH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Ethanal, CH 3 CHO to CH 3 CH 2 OH CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\;{H_2}O}]{{(i)\;LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OH
solution: Propanone oxime the structure of propanone oxime is {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} = N - OH{/tex} Semicarbazone of CH 3 CHO The structure of semicarbazone of CH 3 CHO is {tex}C{H_3} - CH = N - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}{/tex} . Ethanal (CH 3 CHO) to CH 3 -CH 3 CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} HCl \\ Clemmensen \\ reduction \end{subarray} ]{{Zn - Hg}}{/tex} CH 3 -CH 3 + H 2 O Ethanal, (CH 3 CHO) to {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} 2CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} Aldol \\ condensation \end{subarray} ]{{Dil\;NaOH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Ethanal, CH 3 CHO to CH 3 CH 2 OH CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\;{H_2}O}]{{(i)\;LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex} CH 3 CH 2 OH
solution_html:
1. Propanone oxime the structure of propanone oxime is
      {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} = N - OH{/tex}
    2. Semicarbazone of CH3CHO The structure of semicarbazone of CH3 CHO is
      {tex}C{H_3} - CH = N - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - N{H_2}{/tex}.
    1. Ethanal (CH3CHO) to CH3-CH3
      CH3CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} HCl \\ Clemmensen \\ reduction \end{subarray} ]{{Zn - Hg}}{/tex} CH3-CH3 + H2O
    2. Ethanal, (CH3CHO) to {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} 
      2CH3CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[\begin{subarray}{l} Aldol \\ condensation \end{subarray} ]{{Dil\;NaOH}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex}
    3. Ethanal, CH3CHO to CH3CH2OH
      CH3CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\;{H_2}O}]{{(i)\;LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex} CH3CH2OH

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
How do you convert the following?

  1. Benzoic acid to benzaldehyde.
  2. Ethyne to ethanal.
  3. Acetic acid to methane.
question_text: How do you convert the following? Benzoic acid to benzaldehyde. Ethyne to ethanal. Acetic acid to methane.
explanation: Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde Ethyne to ethanal {tex}\mathop {CH \equiv CH}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Elhyn} \end{array}e} \xrightarrow[{{H_2}S{O_4}/HgS{O_4}}]{{{H_2}O}}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} = C{H_2}}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow{{Tautomensation}}\mathop {H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex} Acetic acid to methane {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Acetic\,acid} \xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOH/CaO}}\mathop {C{H_4}}\limits_{Methane} + N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex}
solution: Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde Ethyne to ethanal {tex}\mathop {CH \equiv CH}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Elhyn} \end{array}e} \xrightarrow[{{H_2}S{O_4}/HgS{O_4}}]{{{H_2}O}}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} = C{H_2}}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow{{Tautomensation}}\mathop {H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex} Acetic acid to methane {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Acetic\,acid} \xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOH/CaO}}\mathop {C{H_4}}\limits_{Methane} + N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  2. Ethyne to ethanal
    {tex}\mathop {CH \equiv CH}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Elhyn} \end{array}e} \xrightarrow[{{H_2}S{O_4}/HgS{O_4}}]{{{H_2}O}}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} = C{H_2}}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow{{Tautomensation}}\mathop {H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex}
  3. Acetic acid to methane
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Acetic\,acid} \xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a}\limits_{(unstable)} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{NaOH/CaO}}\mathop {C{H_4}}\limits_{Methane} + N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Predict the products of the following reactions:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\overset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}}{\mathrm C}=\mathrm O\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm i)\mathrm{KOH}/\mathrm{Glycol},\;\Delta}]{(\mathrm i){\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-{\mathrm{NH}}_2} ?{/tex}
  2. {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\mathrm{CO}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}/{\mathrm I}_2}?\;+\;?{/tex}
  3. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}/\mathrm{CaO}}?{/tex}
question_text: Predict the products of the following reactions: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\overset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}}{\mathrm C}=\mathrm O\xrightarrow[{(\mathrm i)\mathrm{KOH}/\mathrm{Glycol},\;\Delta}]{(\mathrm i){\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-{\mathrm{NH}}_2} ?{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\mathrm{CO}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}/{\mathrm I}_2}?\;+\;?{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}/\mathrm{CaO}}?{/tex}
explanation: Carboxyl group of aldehydes or ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_2{/tex} group on treating with hydrazine followed by heating with potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol. It is known as Wolf-kishner reduction. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}=\mathrm O\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{KOH}/\mathrm{glycol},\Delta}]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} Aldehydes and ketones having at least one methyl group linked to carboxyl carbon atom are oxidised by sodium hypohalite ( {tex}{NaOH} / {I}_2{/tex} ) to sodium salts of corresponding carboxylic acids having one carbon atom less than that of carboxyl compound. This is known as haloform reaction. {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\mathrm{CO}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}/{\mathrm I}_2}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\underset{\mathrm{ONa}}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm O}+{\mathrm{CHI}}_3{/tex} Carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide when their sodium salts are heated with sodalime (NaOH and CaO in the ratio of 3 : 1). It is known as decarboxylation reaction. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}/\mathrm{CaO}}{\mathrm{CH}}_4+{\mathrm{Na}}_2{\mathrm{CO}}_3{/tex}
solution: Carboxyl group of aldehydes or ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_2{/tex} group on treating with hydrazine followed by heating with potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol. It is known as Wolf-kishner reduction. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}=\mathrm O\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{KOH}/\mathrm{glycol},\Delta}]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex} Aldehydes and ketones having at least one methyl group linked to carboxyl carbon atom are oxidised by sodium hypohalite ( {tex}{NaOH} / {I}_2{/tex} ) to sodium salts of corresponding carboxylic acids having one carbon atom less than that of carboxyl compound. This is known as haloform reaction. {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\mathrm{CO}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}/{\mathrm I}_2}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\underset{\mathrm{ONa}}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm O}+{\mathrm{CHI}}_3{/tex} Carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide when their sodium salts are heated with sodalime (NaOH and CaO in the ratio of 3 : 1). It is known as decarboxylation reaction. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}/\mathrm{CaO}}{\mathrm{CH}}_4+{\mathrm{Na}}_2{\mathrm{CO}}_3{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Carboxyl group of aldehydes or ketones is reduced to {tex}{CH}_2{/tex} group on treating with hydrazine followed by heating with potassium hydroxide in high boiling solvent such as ethylene glycol. It is known as Wolf-kishner reduction.
    {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}=\mathrm O\xrightarrow[{\mathrm{KOH}/\mathrm{glycol},\Delta}]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}
  2. Aldehydes and ketones having at least one methyl group linked to carboxyl carbon atom are oxidised by sodium hypohalite ({tex}{NaOH} / {I}_2{/tex}) to sodium salts of corresponding carboxylic acids having one carbon atom less than that of carboxyl compound. This is known as haloform reaction.
    {tex}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\mathrm{CO}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3\xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOH}/{\mathrm I}_2}{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5-\underset{\mathrm{ONa}}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm O}+{\mathrm{CHI}}_3{/tex}
  3. Carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide when their sodium salts are heated with sodalime (NaOH and CaO in the ratio of 3 : 1). It is known as decarboxylation reaction.
    {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COONa}\xrightarrow[\mathrm\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}/\mathrm{CaO}}{\mathrm{CH}}_4+{\mathrm{Na}}_2{\mathrm{CO}}_3{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Write the products formed when CH3CHO reacts with the following reagents:
    1. HCN
    2. H2N-OH
    3. CH3CHO in the presence of dilute NaOH
  2. Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:
    1. Benzoic acid and phenol
    2. Propanal and propanone
question_text: Write the products formed when CH 3 CHO reacts with the following reagents: HCN H 2 N-OH CH 3 CHO in the presence of dilute NaOH Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Benzoic acid and phenol Propanal and propanone
explanation: CH 3 CHO + HCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CN}} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin }}\end{array}{/tex} CH 3 CHO + CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[Aldol\ condensation]{Dil\ NaOH}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by the ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl - But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Ferric benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt.) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl Propanal and propanone: These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}+\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array} + 4NH_3 + 2H_2O {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
solution: CH 3 CHO + HCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CN}} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin }}\end{array}{/tex} CH 3 CHO + CH 3 CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[Aldol\ condensation]{Dil\ NaOH}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex} Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by the ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl - But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Ferric benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt.) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl Propanal and propanone: These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}+\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array} + 4NH_3 + 2H_2O {/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
solution_html:
1. CH3CHO + HCN {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CN}} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin }}\end{array}{/tex}
    3. CH3CHO + CH3CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[Aldol\ condensation]{Dil\ NaOH}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_2} - CHO{/tex}
    1. Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by the ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration.
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H+ + 3Cl-
      But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate.
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Ferric benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt.) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl
    2. Propanal and propanone:
      These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not.
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 3OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}+\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array} + 4NH_3 + 2H_2O {/tex}
      {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror

question_html:
How are the following conversions carried out?

  1. Propene to propan-2-ol.
  2. Ethyl chloride to ethanal.
question_text: How are the following conversions carried out? Propene to propan-2-ol. Ethyl chloride to ethanal.
explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH}\limits_{propene} = C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^{||} }\limits_{propan - 2 - ol} }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH_2}\limits_{Ethyl \ chloride} - C{l}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{aq.\ NaOH}}{/tex} CH 3 -CH 2 -OH {tex}\xrightarrow[{Cr{O_3}/PCC}]{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {CHO} \end{array}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH}\limits_{propene} = C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^{||} }\limits_{propan - 2 - ol} }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH_2}\limits_{Ethyl \ chloride} - C{l}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{aq.\ NaOH}}{/tex} CH 3 -CH 2 -OH {tex}\xrightarrow[{Cr{O_3}/PCC}]{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {CHO} \end{array}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH}\limits_{propene} = C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow{{{H_2}O/{H^ + }}}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^{||} }\limits_{propan - 2 - ol} }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2}} \end{array}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {CH_2}\limits_{Ethyl \ chloride} - C{l}} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{aq.\ NaOH}}{/tex} CH3-CH2-OH {tex}\xrightarrow[{Cr{O_3}/PCC}]{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {CHO} \end{array}}\limits_{Ethanal} {/tex}

    2. 2C6H5CHO + conc. NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Benzyl alcohol }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}}{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{a}^{+} } \\ {\text { Sodum benzoate }}\end{array}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following:

  1. CH3CHO is more reactive than CH3COCH3 towards reaction with HCN
  2. Carboxylic acid is a stronger acid than phenol
question_text: Account for the following: CH 3 CHO is more reactive than CH 3 COCH 3 towards reaction with HCN Carboxylic acid is a stronger acid than phenol
explanation: This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH 3 ) in CH 3 CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH 3 ) 2 groups in CH 3 COCH 3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH 3 CHO than in CH 3 COCH 3 . Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH 3 COCH 3 . In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms, whereas in phenoxide ion the negative charge is less effectively delocalised over one oxygen atom and less electronegative carbon atoms. Thus, the carboxylate is more stabilised than phenoxide ion. Hence, carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.
solution: This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH 3 ) in CH 3 CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH 3 ) 2 groups in CH 3 COCH 3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH 3 CHO than in CH 3 COCH 3 . Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH 3 COCH 3 . In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms, whereas in phenoxide ion the negative charge is less effectively delocalised over one oxygen atom and less electronegative carbon atoms. Thus, the carboxylate is more stabilised than phenoxide ion. Hence, carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.

1. This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH3) in CH3CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH3)2 groups in CH3COCH3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH3CHO than in CH3COCH3. Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH3COCH3.
  2. In carboxylate ion, the negative charge is delocalised over two electronegative oxygen atoms, whereas in phenoxide ion the negative charge is less effectively delocalised over one oxygen atom and less electronegative carbon atoms. Thus, the carboxylate is more stabilised than phenoxide ion.
    Hence, carboxylic acids are more acidic than phenols.

question_text: Write the chemical equations to illustrate the following name reactions: Wolff-Kishner reduction Aldol condensation Cannizzaro reaction
explanation: Wolff-Kishner reduction The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to - CH 2 group on treatment with hydrazine followed by heating with KOH in high boiling solvent like ethylene glycol. This reaction is known as Wolff-Kishm;r reduction. {tex}C{H_3}\mathop { - C{H_2}}\limits_{Propane} - C{H_3} + {N_2} \uparrow{/tex} Aldol condensation Aldol condensation In this reaction two molecules of an aldehyde or ketone condense in presence of dilute alkali (dil NaOH, Ba(OH) 2 etc.) to form a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehyde or a {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxyketone respectively. These {tex}\beta{/tex} -hydroxy aldehydes or ketones are collectively called aldols and the reaction is called aldol condensation. The reaction is based on acidity of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones. For eg: Cannizzaro reaction Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to salt of carboxylic acid.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Account for the following :

  1. CH3CHO is more reactive than CH3COCH3 towards reaction with HCN.
  2. There are two -NH2 groups in semicarbazide (H2NNHCONH2). However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazone.
question_text: Account for the following : CH 3 CHO is more reactive than CH 3 COCH 3 towards reaction with HCN. There are two -NH 2 groups in semicarbazide (H 2 NNHCONH 2 ). However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazone.
explanation: This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH 3 ) in CH 3 CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH 3 ) 2 groups in CH 3 COCH 3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH 3 CHO than in CH 3 COCH 3 . Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH 3 COCH 3 . Although semicarbazide has two -NH 2 groups but one of them which is directly attached to C=O group is involved in resonance as shown below: Therefore, the electron density on -NH 2 group involved in the resonance decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. In contrast, the lone pair of electrons on the other -NH 2 group is not involved in resonance, thus, it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones.
solution: This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH 3 ) in CH 3 CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH 3 ) 2 groups in CH 3 COCH 3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH 3 CHO than in CH 3 COCH 3 . Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH 3 COCH 3 . Although semicarbazide has two -NH 2 groups but one of them which is directly attached to C=O group is involved in resonance as shown below: Therefore, the electron density on -NH 2 group involved in the resonance decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. In contrast, the lone pair of electrons on the other -NH 2 group is not involved in resonance, thus, it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones.
solution_html:
1. This is because of the fact that due to smaller +I-effect of one alkyl group (- CH3) in CH3CHO as compared to larger +I-effect of two alkyl (- CH3)2 groups in CH3COCH3 the, magnitude of positive charge on the carbonyl carbon is more in CH3CHO than in CH3COCH3. Also, the steric effect is more pronounced in case of CH3COCH3.
  2. Although semicarbazide has two -NH2 groups but one of them which is directly attached to C=O group is involved in resonance as shown below:

    Therefore, the electron density on -NH2 group involved in the resonance decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. In contrast, the lone pair of electrons on the other -NH2 group is not involved in resonance, thus, it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones.

question_text: Write the chemical equation to illustrate each of the following name reactions: Rosenmund reduction Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction Cannizzaro reaction
explanation: Rosemund reduction {tex}\mathop {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - Cl} \end{array}}\limits_{Ethanoyl\,chloride} {/tex} + H 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{Pd\ BaSO_4}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + HCl Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ)Reaction Carboxylic acids having {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom are halogenated at the a-position on treatment with chlorine or bromine in the presence of small amount of red phosphorus to give {tex}\alpha{/tex} -halocarboxylic acids. The reaction is known as Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction. R-CH 2 -COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii){H_2}O}]{{(i){X_2},\operatorname{Re} d\,phosphorus}}{/tex} {tex}R\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H-}\limits_X }\limits_{\alpha - halocarboxyhc{\text{ }}acid}COOH{/tex} (X = Cl Br) Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid.

The reaction is known as Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction. R-CH 2 -COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii){H_2}O}]{{(i){X_2},\operatorname{Re} d\,phosphorus}}{/tex} {tex}R\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H-}\limits_X }\limits_{\alpha - halocarboxyhc{\text{ }}acid}COOH{/tex} (X = Cl Br) Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid.

1. Rosemund reduction
    {tex}\mathop {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - Cl} \end{array}}\limits_{Ethanoyl\,chloride} {/tex}     + H2 {tex}\xrightarrow{Pd\ BaSO_4}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + HCl
  2. Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ)Reaction Carboxylic acids having {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atom are halogenated at the a-position on treatment with chlorine or bromine in the presence of small amount of red phosphorus to give {tex}\alpha{/tex}-halocarboxylic acids. The reaction is known as Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction.
    R-CH2-COOH {tex}\xrightarrow[{(ii){H_2}O}]{{(i){X_2},\operatorname{Re} d\,phosphorus}}{/tex} {tex}R\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H-}\limits_X }\limits_{\alpha - halocarboxyhc{\text{ }}acid}COOH{/tex} (X = Cl Br)
  3. Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid.
