  2. Give tests to distinguish between the following pair of compound: Ethanal and propanal.
question_text: Draw the structures of the following compounds: 4-chloropentan-2-one p-nitropropiophenone Give tests to distinguish between the following pair of compound: Ethanal and propanal.
explanation: {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^1 {H_3} - \mathop C\limits^2 O - \mathop C\limits^3 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^4 H(Cl) - \mathop C\limits^5 {H_3}}\limits_{4 - chloropen\tan - 2 - one}{/tex} Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence of CH 3 CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution: {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^1 {H_3} - \mathop C\limits^2 O - \mathop C\limits^3 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^4 H(Cl) - \mathop C\limits^5 {H_3}}\limits_{4 - chloropen\tan - 2 - one}{/tex} Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence of CH 3 CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^1 {H_3} - \mathop C\limits^2 O - \mathop C\limits^3 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^4 H(Cl) - \mathop C\limits^5 {H_3}}\limits_{4 - chloropen\tan - 2 - one}{/tex}

    1. Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal
      Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence of CH3CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test.
      {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex}+ {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 3H2O
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  No reaction

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:

  1. Phenol and benzoic acid
  2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone
question_text: Give test to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Phenol and benzoic acid Benzaldehyde and acetophenone
explanation: Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl| But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution: Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl| But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction
solution_html:
1. Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol
    Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 6H+ + 3Cl|
    But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl
  2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone 
    Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent.
       {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex}
     {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction

question_html: An organic compound (A) which has characteristic odour, on treatment with NaOH forms two compounds (B) and (C). Compound (B)has the molecular formula C7H8O which on oxidation with CrO3 gives back compound (A). Compound (C) is the sodium salt of the acid. Compound (C) when heated with soda lime yields an aromatic hydrocarbon (D). Deduce the structures of (A),(B),(C) and (D). Write chemical equations for all reaction taking place.
question_text: An organic compound (A) which has characteristic odour, on treatment with NaOH forms two compounds (B) and (C). Compound (B)has the molecular formula C 7 H 8 O which on oxidation with CrO 3 gives back compound (A). Compound (C) is the sodium salt of the acid. Compound (C) when heated with soda lime yields an aromatic hydrocarbon (D). Deduce the structures of (A),(B),(C) and (D). Write chemical equations for all reaction taking place.
explanation: Compound 'A' has characteristic odour that means it can be benzaldehyde, which undergo cannizzaro reaction with NaOH to give alcohol and sodium salt of acid. Compound 'B' can be alcohol as it contain only one oxygen atom which on oxidation gives back compound 'A'. Compound 'C' can be sodium salt of benzoic acid which on heating with sodalime yields aromatic compound 'D', which may be benzene. Reaction involved are given below
solution: Compound 'A' has characteristic odour that means it can be benzaldehyde, which undergo cannizzaro reaction with NaOH to give alcohol and sodium salt of acid. Compound 'B' can be alcohol as it contain only one oxygen atom which on oxidation gives back compound 'A'. Compound 'C' can be sodium salt of benzoic acid which on heating with sodalime yields aromatic compound 'D', which may be benzene. Reaction involved are given below

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds:

  1. Ethanal and propanal.
  2. Benzoic acid and phenol.
question_text: Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds: Ethanal and propanal. Benzoic acid and phenol.
explanation: Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence ofCH3CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl| But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl
solution: Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence ofCH3CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test. {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 6H + + 3Cl| But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl 3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl 3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl
solution_html:
1. Distinguishing test between ethanal and propanal
    Iodoform test Ethanal because of the presence ofCH3CO- skeleton gives positive iodoform test whereas propanal due to the absence of such a skeleton does not gives such test.
    {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Ethanal }} \\ {\text { Or }} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }} \\ {\text { Iodoform }}\end{array}{/tex}+ {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { HCO } \overline{\mathrm{N}}^{+} \text {a }} \\ {\text { Sodium formate }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\text { 3NaI }} \\ {\text { Sodıum }} \\ {\text { ıodade }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 3H2O
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\quad \text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NaOH +3I2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex}  No reaction
  2. Distinguishing test between benzoic acid and phenol
    Phenol and benzoic acid can be distinguished by ferric chloride test. Phenol reacts with neutral FeCl3 to form ferric phenoxide complex giving violet colouration.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{6 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}} \\ {\text { Phenol }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{6}\right]^{3^-}} \\ {\text { Iron-phenol complex }} \\ {\text { (Violet colour) }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 6H+ + 3Cl|
    But benzoic acid reacts with neutral FeCl3 to give a buff coloured precipitate of ferric benzoate.
    {tex}\begin{array}{c}{3 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}} \\ {\text { Benzoic acid }}\end{array}{/tex} + FeCl3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Fe}} \\ {\text { Fernc benzoate }} \\ {\text { (Buff coloured ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3HCl

question_text: How will you convert the following: Propanone to propan-2-ol? Ethanal to 2-hydroxypropanoic acid? Toluene to benzoic acid? Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between pentan-2-one and pentan-3-one ethanal and propanal
explanation: Propanone to propan-2-ol {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opanone} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Reduction]{LiAI_4}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opan - 2 - ol} \end{array}} {/tex} Ethanal to 2-hydroxypropanoic acid Toluene to benzoic acid Pentan-2-one and pentan-3-one can be distinguished by iodoform test. On heating with NaOH + I 2 or [NaOI], propan-2-one being a methyl ketone forms yellow ppt of iodoform, whereas pentan-3-one does not. {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH {tex}{C{H_3} CH_2- \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - CH_2C{H_3}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NaOI}{/tex} No yellow ppt of Iodoform Ethanal and propana l Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - }{/tex} group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate ofCHI 3 . While propanal does not give this test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Ethanal} \end{array}} {/tex} +3NaOI {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex} + 2NaOH + CHI 3 {tex}\xrightarrow{(yellow\ ppt)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOI}{/tex} Yellow ppt of CHI 3
solution: Propanone to propan-2-ol {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opanone} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Reduction]{LiAI_4}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opan - 2 - ol} \end{array}} {/tex} Ethanal to 2-hydroxypropanoic acid Toluene to benzoic acid Pentan-2-one and pentan-3-one can be distinguished by iodoform test. On heating with NaOH + I 2 or [NaOI], propan-2-one being a methyl ketone forms yellow ppt of iodoform, whereas pentan-3-one does not. {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH {tex}{C{H_3} CH_2- \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - CH_2C{H_3}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NaOI}{/tex} No yellow ppt of Iodoform Ethanal and propana l Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - }{/tex} group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate ofCHI 3 . While propanal does not give this test. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Ethanal} \end{array}} {/tex} +3NaOI {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex} + 2NaOH + CHI 3 {tex}\xrightarrow{(yellow\ ppt)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOI}{/tex} Yellow ppt of CHI 3
solution_html:
1.  
    1. Propanone to propan-2-ol
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opanone} \end{array}} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[Reduction]{LiAI_4}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{OH} H - C{H_3}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\Pr opan - 2 - ol} \end{array}} {/tex}
    2. Ethanal to 2-hydroxypropanoic acid
    3. Toluene to benzoic acid
  2.  
    1. Pentan-2-one and pentan-3-one can be distinguished by iodoform test.
      On heating with NaOH + I2 or [NaOI], propan-2-one being a methyl ketone forms yellow ppt of iodoform, whereas pentan-3-one does not.
      {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH
      {tex}{C{H_3} CH_2- \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - CH_2C{H_3}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{NaOI}{/tex} No yellow ppt of Iodoform
    2. Ethanal and propanal Both can be distinguished by iodoform test. Ethanal contains {tex}{C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - }{/tex} group, therefore, it undergoes iodoform reaction and gives yellow precipitate ofCHI3. While propanal does not give this test.
      {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {Ethanal} \end{array}} {/tex} +3NaOI {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CO\mathop O\limits^ - N\mathop a\limits^ + \,{/tex}+ 2NaOH + CHI3 {tex}\xrightarrow{(yellow\ ppt)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3} - CHO}\limits_{\Pr opanal} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOI}{/tex} Yellow ppt of CHI3

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions.

  1. Ethanol, Propanal, Propanone, Butanone
  2. Benzaldehyde, p-Tolualdehyde, p-Nitrobenzaldehyde, Acetophenone.

Hint: Consider steric effect and electronic effect.
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions. Ethanol, Propanal, Propanone, Butanone Benzaldehyde, p-Tolualdehyde, p-Nitrobenzaldehyde, Acetophenone. Hint: Consider steric effect and electronic effect.
explanation: The +I effect of the alkyl group increases in the order: Ethanal < Propanal < Propanone < Butanone The electron density at the carbonyl carbon increases with the increase in the +I effect of the alkyl group. As a result, the chances of attack by a nucleophile decrease. Hence, the increasing order is: Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanol The + I effect is more in ketone than in aldehyde. Hence, acetophenone is the least reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions. Among aldehydes, the +I effect is the highest in p- tolualdehyde because of the presence of the electron-donating -CH 3 group and the lowest in p -nitrobenzaldehyde because of the presence of the electron-withdrawing -NO 2 group. Hence, the increasing order is: Acetophenone < p-Tolualdehyde < Benzaldehyde < p-Nitrobenzaldehyde
solution: The +I effect of the alkyl group increases in the order: Ethanal < Propanal < Propanone < Butanone The electron density at the carbonyl carbon increases with the increase in the +I effect of the alkyl group. As a result, the chances of attack by a nucleophile decrease. Hence, the increasing order is: Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanol The + I effect is more in ketone than in aldehyde. Hence, acetophenone is the least reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions. Among aldehydes, the +I effect is the highest in p- tolualdehyde because of the presence of the electron-donating -CH 3 group and the lowest in p -nitrobenzaldehyde because of the presence of the electron-withdrawing -NO 2 group. Hence, the increasing order is: Acetophenone < p-Tolualdehyde < Benzaldehyde < p-Nitrobenzaldehyde
solution_html:
1. The +I effect of the alkyl group increases in the order: Ethanal < Propanal < Propanone < Butanone
    The electron density at the carbonyl carbon increases with the increase in the +I effect of the alkyl group. As a result, the chances of attack by a nucleophile decrease. Hence, the increasing order is:
    Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanol


  2. The + I effect is more in ketone than in aldehyde. Hence, acetophenone is the least reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions.
    Among aldehydes, the +I effect is the highest in p-tolualdehyde because of the presence of the electron-donating  -CH3 group and the lowest in p-nitrobenzaldehyde because of the presence of the electron-withdrawing -NO2 group. Hence, the increasing order is:
    Acetophenone < p-Tolualdehyde < Benzaldehyde < p-Nitrobenzaldehyde

question_html:
Give plausible explanation for each of the following:

  1. Cyclohexanone forms cyanohydrin in good yield but 2, 2, 6 trimethylcyclohexanone does not.
  2. There are two -NH2 groups in semicarbazide. However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazones.
  3. During the preparation of esters from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst, the water or the ester should be removed as soon as it is formed.
question_text: Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Cyclohexanone forms cyanohydrin in good yield but 2, 2, 6 trimethylcyclohexanone does not. There are two -NH 2 groups in semicarbazide. However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazones. During the preparation of esters from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst, the water or the ester should be removed as soon as it is formed.
explanation: Cyclohexanones form cyanohydrins according to the following equation. In this case, the nucleophile CN - can easily attack without any steric hindrance. However, in the case of 2, 2, 6 trimethylcydohexanone, methyl groups at 2,2,6 positions offer steric hindrances and as a result, CN - cannot attack effectively. For this reason, it does not form a cyanohydrin. Semicarbazide undergoes resonance involving only one of the two - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} groups, which is attached directly to the carbonyl-carbon atom. Therefore, the electron density on - NH 2 group involved in the resonance also decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. Since the other - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} group is not involved in resonance; it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl-carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones. Ester along with water is formed reversibly from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in presence of an acid. {tex}\mathop {RCOOH}\limits_{Carboxylic\,acid} + \mathop {R'OH}\limits_{Alcohol} \overset {{H^ + }} \longleftrightarrow \mathop {RCOOR'}\limits_{Ester} + \mathop {{H_2O}}\limits_{water}{/tex} If either water or ester is not removed as soon as it is formed, then it reacts to give back the reactants as the reaction is reversible. Therefore, to shift the equilibrium in the forward direction i.e., to produce more ester, either of the two products should be removed (Le-Chateliers Principle).
solution: Cyclohexanones form cyanohydrins according to the following equation. In this case, the nucleophile CN - can easily attack without any steric hindrance. However, in the case of 2, 2, 6 trimethylcydohexanone, methyl groups at 2,2,6 positions offer steric hindrances and as a result, CN - cannot attack effectively. For this reason, it does not form a cyanohydrin. Semicarbazide undergoes resonance involving only one of the two - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} groups, which is attached directly to the carbonyl-carbon atom. Therefore, the electron density on - NH 2 group involved in the resonance also decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. Since the other - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} group is not involved in resonance; it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl-carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones. Ester along with water is formed reversibly from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in presence of an acid. {tex}\mathop {RCOOH}\limits_{Carboxylic\,acid} + \mathop {R'OH}\limits_{Alcohol} \overset {{H^ + }} \longleftrightarrow \mathop {RCOOR'}\limits_{Ester} + \mathop {{H_2O}}\limits_{water}{/tex} If either water or ester is not removed as soon as it is formed, then it reacts to give back the reactants as the reaction is reversible. Therefore, to shift the equilibrium in the forward direction i.e., to produce more ester, either of the two products should be removed (Le-Chateliers Principle).
solution_html:
1. Cyclohexanones form cyanohydrins according to the following equation.

    In this case, the nucleophile CN- can easily attack without any steric hindrance. However, in the case of 2, 2, 6 trimethylcydohexanone, methyl groups at 2,2,6 positions offer steric hindrances and as a result, CN- cannot attack effectively.

    For this reason, it does not form a cyanohydrin.
  2. Semicarbazide undergoes resonance involving only one of the two - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} groups, which is attached directly to the carbonyl-carbon atom.

    Therefore, the electron density on -  NH2group involved in the resonance also decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. Since the other - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} group is not involved in resonance; it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl-carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones.
  3. Ester along with water is formed reversibly from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in presence of an acid.
    {tex}\mathop {RCOOH}\limits_{Carboxylic\,acid} + \mathop {R'OH}\limits_{Alcohol} \overset {{H^ + }} \longleftrightarrow \mathop {RCOOR'}\limits_{Ester} + \mathop {{H_2O}}\limits_{water}{/tex}
    If either water or ester is not removed as soon as it is formed, then it reacts to give back the reactants as the reaction is reversible. Therefore, to shift the equilibrium in the forward direction i.e., to produce more ester, either of the two products should be removed (Le-Chateliers Principle).

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds.

  1. Propanoyl chloride and propanoic acid
  2. Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone
question_text: Give chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds. Propanoyl chloride and propanoic acid Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone
explanation: On adding NaHCO 3 solution to each of them, propanoyl chloride will not react whereas propanoic acid will give brisk effervescence due to CO 2 . {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-Cl\;\;+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow\;No\;reaction{/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-OH+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-\overset-{O\;}N\overset+a\;+\;H_2O\;+\;CO_2{/tex} On adding I 2 and NaOH, Acetophenone will give yellow ppt of iodoform whereas benzaldehyde will not react. Benzaldehyde + I 2 + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} No reaction {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Acetophenone} \; + \;\mathop {3NaOH}\limits_{Sodium\;hypoiodite} \; \to \;\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COONa\;}\limits_{Sodium\;benzoate} + \;\mathop {CH{I_3}\;}\limits_{Iodoform(yellow\;ppt)} + 2NaOH{/tex}
solution: On adding NaHCO 3 solution to each of them, propanoyl chloride will not react whereas propanoic acid will give brisk effervescence due to CO 2 . {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-Cl\;\;+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow\;No\;reaction{/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-OH+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-\overset-{O\;}N\overset+a\;+\;H_2O\;+\;CO_2{/tex} On adding I 2 and NaOH, Acetophenone will give yellow ppt of iodoform whereas benzaldehyde will not react. Benzaldehyde + I 2 + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} No reaction {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Acetophenone} \; + \;\mathop {3NaOH}\limits_{Sodium\;hypoiodite} \; \to \;\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COONa\;}\limits_{Sodium\;benzoate} + \;\mathop {CH{I_3}\;}\limits_{Iodoform(yellow\;ppt)} + 2NaOH{/tex}
solution_html:
1. On adding NaHCO3 solution to each of them, propanoyl chloride will not react whereas propanoic acid will give brisk effervescence due to CO2.
    {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-Cl\;\;+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow\;No\;reaction{/tex}

    {tex}CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-OH+\;NaHCO_3\;\rightarrow CH_3-CH_2-\overset O{\overset{\vert\vert}C}-\overset-{O\;}N\overset+a\;+\;H_2O\;+\;CO_2{/tex}
     
  2. On adding I2 and NaOH, Acetophenone will give yellow ppt of iodoform whereas benzaldehyde will not react.

         Benzaldehyde + I2 + NaOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} No reaction
    {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COC{H_3}}\limits_{Acetophenone} \; + \;\mathop {3NaOH}\limits_{Sodium\;hypoiodite} \; \to \;\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COONa\;}\limits_{Sodium\;benzoate} + \;\mathop {CH{I_3}\;}\limits_{Iodoform(yellow\;ppt)} + 2NaOH{/tex}

question_html: Give chemical test to distinguish between benzophenone and acetophenone.
question_text: Give chemical test to distinguish between benzophenone and acetophenone.
explanation: Benzophenone (C 6 H 5 COC 6 H 5 ) and acetophenone (C 6 H 5 COCH 3 ) can be distinguished by the iodoform test. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetophenone }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a} \end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}{/tex} {tex}\stackrel{\mathrm{NaOl}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} No yellow ppt. of iodoform
solution: Benzophenone (C 6 H 5 COC 6 H 5 ) and acetophenone (C 6 H 5 COCH 3 ) can be distinguished by the iodoform test. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetophenone }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a} \end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH {tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}{/tex} {tex}\stackrel{\mathrm{NaOl}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex} No yellow ppt. of iodoform
solution_html:
Benzophenone (C6H5COC6H5) and acetophenone (C6H5COCH3) can be distinguished by the iodoform test.
{tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Acetophenone }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3NaOI {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{C_6}{H_5}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a} \end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Iodoform }} \\ {\text { (Yellow ppt) }}\end{array}{/tex} + 2NaOH 
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COC}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}{/tex} {tex}\stackrel{\mathrm{NaOl}}{\longrightarrow}{/tex}  No yellow ppt. of iodoform

1. Describe the mechanism of the addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group of a compound to form an adduct which on hydrolysis yield an alcohol.
  2. Draw the structure of the following compounds:

    2. p-nitropropiophenone
question_text: Describe the mechanism of the addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group of a compound to form an adduct which on hydrolysis yield an alcohol. Draw the structure of the following compounds: 3-methylbutanal p-nitropropiophenone
explanation: Alcohols are produced by the addition reaction of Grignard's reagents with the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones. Mechanism Step I The first step of the reaction is the nucleophilic addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct. Step II Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol. 3-methylbutanal {tex}{{H_3}{}^4C - \mathop {\mathop {{}^3C}\limits^| }\limits^{C{H_3}} H - {}^2C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop {{}^1C}\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}{/tex} p-nitropropiophenone
solution: Alcohols are produced by the addition reaction of Grignard's reagents with the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones. Mechanism Step I The first step of the reaction is the nucleophilic addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct. Step II Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol. 3-methylbutanal {tex}{{H_3}{}^4C - \mathop {\mathop {{}^3C}\limits^| }\limits^{C{H_3}} H - {}^2C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop {{}^1C}\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}{/tex} p-nitropropiophenone
solution_html:
1. Alcohols are produced by the addition reaction of Grignard's reagents with the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones.
    Mechanism
    Step I The first step of the reaction is the nucleophilic addition of Grignard's reagent to the carbonyl group to form an adduct.

    Step II Hydrolysis of the adduct yields an alcohol.
  2.  
    1. 3-methylbutanal

    2. p-nitropropiophenone

question_html:
1. Illustrate the following reaction giving suitable chemical equations: Cannizzaro reaction.
  2. How would you bring about the following conversions? Write the complete equations in each case.
    1. Ethanal to 3-hydroxybutanal
    2. Benzaldehydeto benzophenone
question_text: Illustrate the following reaction giving suitable chemical equations: Cannizzaro reaction. How would you bring about the following conversions? Write the complete equations in each case. Ethanal to 3-hydroxybutanal Benzaldehydeto benzophenone
explanation: Clemmensen reduction The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH 2 group on treatment with zinc-amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid. This reaction is known as Clemmensen reduction. e.g. . Ethanal to 3-hydroxybutanal {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} - CHO{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^4 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits^3 }\limits_| }\limits_{OH} H - \mathop C\limits^2 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^1 HO}\limits_{3 - hydroxybutanal} {/tex} Benzaldehyde to benzophenone
solution: Clemmensen reduction The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH 2 group on treatment with zinc-amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid. This reaction is known as Clemmensen reduction. e.g. . Ethanal to 3-hydroxybutanal {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} - CHO{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^4 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits^3 }\limits_| }\limits_{OH} H - \mathop C\limits^2 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^1 HO}\limits_{3 - hydroxybutanal} {/tex} Benzaldehyde to benzophenone
solution_html:
1. Clemmensen reduction The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is reduced to -CH2 group on treatment with zinc-amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid. This reaction is known as Clemmensen reduction.

    e.g.  .
    1. Ethanal to 3-hydroxybutanal
      {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethanal} - CHO{/tex}   {tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits^4 {H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits^3 }\limits_| }\limits_{OH} H - \mathop C\limits^2 {H_2} - \mathop C\limits^1 HO}\limits_{3 - hydroxybutanal} {/tex}
    2. Benzaldehyde to benzophenone

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated:

  1. Acetaldehyde, Acetone, Di-tert-butyl ketone, Methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN)
  2. CH3CH2CH(Br)COOH, CH3CH(Br)CH2COOH, (CH3)2CHCOOH, CH3CH2CH2COOH (acid strength)
  3. Benzoic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxybenzoic acid (acid strength)
question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated: Acetaldehyde, Acetone, Di-tert-butyl ketone, Methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN) CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH, CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH, (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH, CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH (acid strength) Benzoic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxybenzoic acid (acid strength)
explanation: When HCN reacts with a compound, the attacking species is a nucleophile, CN - . Therefore, as the negative charge on the compound increases, its reactivity with HCN decreases. In the given compounds, the +I effect increases as shown below. It can be observed that steric hindrance also increases in the same manner (no of alkyl groups). Hence, the given compounds can be arranged according to their increasing reactivities toward HCN as: Di-tert-butyl ketone < Methyl tert-butyl ketone < Acetone < Acetaldehyde After losing a proton, carboxylic acids gain a negative charge as shown: {tex}R - COOH \to R - CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} Now, any group that will help stabilise the negative charge will increase the stability of the carboxylate ion and as a result, will increase the strength of the acid. Thus, groups having +I effect will decrease the strength of the acids and groups having - I effect will increase the strength of the acids. In the given compounds, -CH 3 group has +I effect and Br - group has - I effect. Thus, acids containing Br - are stronger. Now, the +I effect of isopropyl group is more than that of n-propyl group. Hence, (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH is a weaker acid than CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH. Also, the - I effect grows weaker as distance increases. Hence, CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH is a weaker acid than CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as: (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH < CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH < CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH < CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH. As we have seen in the previous case, electron-donating groups decrease the strengths of acids, while electron-withdrawing groups increase the strengths of acids. As methoxy group is an electron-donating group, 4-methoxybenzoic acid is a weaker acid than benzoic acid. Nitro group is an electron-withdrawing group and will increase the strengths of acids. As 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid contains two nitro groups, it is a slightly stronger acid than 4-nitrobenzoic acid. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as: 4-Methoxybenzoic acid < Benzoic acid < 4-Nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid.

  3. As we have seen in the previous case, electron-donating groups decrease the strengths of acids, while electron-withdrawing groups increase the strengths of acids. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. An organic compound with molecular formula C9H10O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, reduces Tollen's reagent and undergoes Cannizzaro reaction. On vigorous oxidation, it gives 1, 2-benzene dicarboxylic acid. Identify the compound.
  2. Give the chemical tests to distinguish between
    1. propanol and propanone
    2. benzaldehyde and acetophenone
  3. Arrange the following compounds in an increasing order of their property as indicated:
    Acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN).
question_text: An organic compound with molecular formula C 9 H 10 O forms 2,4-DNP derivative, reduces Tollen's reagent and undergoes Cannizzaro reaction. On vigorous oxidation, it gives 1, 2-benzene dicarboxylic acid. Identify the compound. Give the chemical tests to distinguish between propanol and propanone benzaldehyde and acetophenone Arrange the following compounds in an increasing order of their property as indicated: Acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN).
explanation: Since, the compound gives 2,4-DNP derivative, it contains group. It reduces Tollen's reagent, that means, the carbonyl compound is an aldehyde (-CHO group is present). It gives Cannizzaro reaction, so it does not contain any {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Illustrate the following reaction giving a suitable example:
Cross aldol condensation
question_text: Illustrate the following reaction giving a suitable example: Cross aldol condensation
explanation: Cross aldol condensation: When aldol condensation is carried out between two different aldehydes or ketones or between an aldehyde or a ketone and at least one of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H, it is called cross aldol condensation. If both of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atoms, it gives a mixture of four products.
solution: Cross aldol condensation: When aldol condensation is carried out between two different aldehydes or ketones or between an aldehyde or a ketone and at least one of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H, it is called cross aldol condensation. If both of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atoms, it gives a mixture of four products.

If both of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atoms, it gives a mixture of four products.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: An organic compound with molecular formula C5H10O does not-reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and gives a positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation, it gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid. Identify the compound and write all chemical equations for the reactions.
question_text: An organic compound with molecular formula C 5 H 10 O does not-reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and gives a positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation, it gives ethanoic acid and propanoic acid. Identify the compound and write all chemical equations for the reactions.
explanation: Given organic compound C 9 H 10 O does not reduce Tollen's reagent, so it is not an aldehyde but the formation of addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite indicates it to be a carbonyl compound. It is also given that it gives positive iodoform test so it should contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group. So, it must be aketon. The possible structure may be {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits_I - C{H_2}C{H_3}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } \mathop { - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } H}\limits_{II} - C{H_3}{/tex} Further given compound on oxidation gives ethanoic and propanoic acid and which is possible when the compound is I. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Ethanoic\,\,acid} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Propanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}
solution: Given organic compound C 9 H 10 O does not reduce Tollen's reagent, so it is not an aldehyde but the formation of addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite indicates it to be a carbonyl compound. It is also given that it gives positive iodoform test so it should contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group. So, it must be aketon. The possible structure may be {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits_I - C{H_2}C{H_3}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } \mathop { - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } H}\limits_{II} - C{H_3}{/tex} Further given compound on oxidation gives ethanoic and propanoic acid and which is possible when the compound is I. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Ethanoic\,\,acid} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Propanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given organic compound C9H10O does not reduce Tollen's reagent, so it is not an aldehyde but the formation of addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite indicates it to be a carbonyl compound. It is also given that it gives positive iodoform test so it should contain {tex} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - C{H_3}{/tex} group. So, it must be aketon. The possible structure may be
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits_I - C{H_2}C{H_3}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } \mathop { - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } H}\limits_{II} - C{H_3}{/tex}
Further given compound on oxidation gives ethanoic and propanoic acid and which is possible when the compound is I.
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{[O]}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{Ethanoic\,\,acid} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Propanoic\,\,acid}{/tex}

    1. CH3COCH3 {tex}\xrightarrow[{conc\,\,HCl}]{{Zn.Hg}}{/tex}

  2. Which acid of each pair shown here would you expect to be stronger acid?

    2.  or CH3COOH
question_text: Predict the products CH 3 COCH 3 {tex}\xrightarrow[{conc\,\,HCl}]{{Zn.Hg}}{/tex} CH 3 COCl + H 2 {tex}\xrightarrow{{Pd \cdot BaS{O_4}}}{/tex} Which acid of each pair shown here would you expect to be stronger acid? F-CH 2 -COOH or Cl-CH 2 -COOH or CH 3 COOH
explanation: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{Acetone} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Zn - Hg}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{Propane} + {H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Pd - BaS{O_4}}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCl{/tex} F- CH 2 - COOH is a stronger acid than Cl-CH 2 -COOH because, higher the -I effect, stronger is the acid. The order of -I effect is I< Br < Cl < F. CH 3 COOH is a stronger acid than phenol,
solution: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{Acetone} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Zn - Hg}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{Propane} + {H_2}O{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Pd - BaS{O_4}}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCl{/tex} F- CH 2 - COOH is a stronger acid than Cl-CH 2 -COOH because, higher the -I effect, stronger is the acid. The order of -I effect is I< Br < Cl < F. CH 3 COOH is a stronger acid than phenol,
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - C{H_3}}\limits_{Acetone} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Zn - Hg}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{Propane} + {H_2}O{/tex}
    2. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - Cl + {H_2}}\limits_{Acetyl\,\,chloride} \mathop {\xrightarrow{{Pd - BaS{O_4}}}}\limits_{\mathop {(Rosenmund}\limits_{reduction)} } \mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCl{/tex}
    1. F- CH2 - COOH is a stronger acid than Cl-CH2-COOH because, higher the -I effect, stronger is the acid. The order of -I effect is I< Br < Cl < F.
    2. CH3COOH is a stronger acid than phenol,

question_text: Give chemical tests to distinguish between propanal and propanone benzaldehyde and acetophenone How would you obtain butanoic acid from butanol? benzoic acid from ethyl benzene?
explanation: Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NH 3 + 2H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{Butan ol} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,Dil\,\,{H_2}S{O_4}}]{{(i)\,\,{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Butan oic\,\,acid} {/tex}
solution: Propanal and propanone These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not. {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex} + 3OH - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} + 4NH 3 + 2H 2 O {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{Butan ol} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,Dil\,\,{H_2}S{O_4}}]{{(i)\,\,{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Butan oic\,\,acid} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Propanal and propanone
      These compounds can be distinguished by using Tollen's test. Propanal being an aldehyde reduces Tollen's reagent to shining silver mirror and propanone being a ketone does not.
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {\text { Propanal }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{l}{2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}} \\ {\text {Tollen's reagent }}\end{array}{/tex}+ 3OH- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COO}^{-}} \\ {\text {Propanoate ion }}\end{array}{/tex} + {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{Ag} \downarrow} \\ {\text { Silver mirror }}\end{array}{/tex} +
      4NH3 + 2H2O 
      {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}} \\ {\text { Propanone }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{reagent}]{{Tollen's}}{/tex} No silver mirror
    2. Benzaldehyde and acetophenone Both can be distinguished by Tollen' s reagent test. Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent.
       {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex}
       {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction.

question_text: Illustrate the following name reaction: Cannizzaro's reaction How would you obtain butanoic acid from butanol? benzoic acid from ethyl benzene?
explanation: Cannizzaro's reaction: Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{Butan ol} \xrightarrow[{(ii)\,Dil\,\,{H_2}S{O_4}}]{{(i)\,\,{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}COOH}\limits_{Butan oic\,\,acid} {/tex}

Benzaldehyde contains - CHO group and thus, reduces Tollen's reagent to metallic silver while acetophenone being a ketone does not reduce Tollen's reagent. {tex}\mathop { + 2Ag \downarrow }\limits_{\mathop {(Silver}\limits_{mirror)} } + 2{H_2}O + 4N{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} No reaction C 6 H 5 CHO + H 2 NCONHNH 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C 6 H 5 CH=NNHCONH 2

question_html: A compound A(C2H6O) on oxidation by PCC gave B, which on treatment with aqueous alkali and subsequent heating furnished C. Bon oxidation by KMnO4, forms a monobasic carboxylic acid with molar mass 60 g mol-1. Deduce the structures of A, B and C.
question_text: A compound A(C 2 H 6 O) on oxidation by PCC gave B, which on treatment with aqueous alkali and subsequent heating furnished C. Bon oxidation by KMnO 4 , forms a monobasic carboxylic acid with molar mass 60 g mol -1 . Deduce the structures of A, B and C.
explanation: Monobasic carboxylic acid = RCOOH.Given that, molar mass of RCOOH = 60 grnol -1 i.e. x + 12+ 16 + 16 + 1 = 60; x = 15 Thus, R = -CH 3 (molar mass 15) and the acid is CH 3 COOH. Since, the acid is obtained by the oxidation of aldehyde, so B is an aldehyde, i.e. CH 3 CHO and A is CH 3 CH 2 OH as it gives B on oxidation by PCC. C is an alkene as it is formed by subsequent heating of aldehyde (B) with aqueous alkali. Thus, the involved reactions are as follows: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_A \xrightarrow{{PCC}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - OH}\limits_{\mathop {(Molecular\,Weight,}\limits_{60\,g\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}})} }{/tex} {tex}2C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{Condensation} }]{{O{H^ - }}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } H - C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{Heat}}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_C CH - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex}
solution: Monobasic carboxylic acid = RCOOH.Given that, molar mass of RCOOH = 60 grnol -1 i.e. x + 12+ 16 + 16 + 1 = 60; x = 15 Thus, R = -CH 3 (molar mass 15) and the acid is CH 3 COOH. Since, the acid is obtained by the oxidation of aldehyde, so B is an aldehyde, i.e. CH 3 CHO and A is CH 3 CH 2 OH as it gives B on oxidation by PCC. C is an alkene as it is formed by subsequent heating of aldehyde (B) with aqueous alkali. Thus, the involved reactions are as follows: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_A \xrightarrow{{PCC}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - OH}\limits_{\mathop {(Molecular\,Weight,}\limits_{60\,g\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}})} }{/tex} {tex}2C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{Condensation} }]{{O{H^ - }}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } H - C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{Heat}}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_C CH - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex}
solution_html:
Monobasic carboxylic acid = RCOOH.Given that, molar mass of RCOOH = 60 grnol-1 
i.e. x + 12+ 16 + 16 + 1 = 60; x = 15
Thus, R = -CH3(molar mass 15) and the acid is CH3COOH. Since, the acid is obtained by the oxidation of aldehyde, so B is an aldehyde, i.e. CH3CHO and A is CH3CH2OH as it gives B on oxidation by PCC. C is an alkene as it is formed by subsequent heating of aldehyde (B) with aqueous alkali. Thus, the involved reactions are as follows:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_A \xrightarrow{{PCC}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - OH}\limits_{\mathop {(Molecular\,Weight,}\limits_{60\,g\,\,mo{l^{ - 1}})} }{/tex}
{tex}2C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_B^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{Condensation} }]{{O{H^ - }}}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } H - C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{Heat}}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_C CH - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H{/tex}

question_text: Describe the following giving linked chemical equations: Cannizzaro reaction Decarboxylation Complete the following chemical equations: C 6 H 5 CONH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[{Heat}]{{{H_3}{O^ + }}}{/tex}
explanation: Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid. Decarboxylation refers to the reaction in which carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide to form hydrocarbons when their sodium salt are heated with soda lime. e.g. {tex} \begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{Na^+}} \\ {\text { sodium }} \\ {\text { ehanoate }}\end{array} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{Sodalime{\text{ }}(mixture{\text{ }}of{\text{ }}NaOH\,and\,Ca0\,\,in\,3{\text{ and 1 }}ratio)}}{/tex} {tex} \begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{4}} \\ {\text { Methane }}\end{array} {/tex} + Na 2 CO 3 C 6 H 5 CONH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{{H_3}{O^ + }}}{/tex} C 6 H 5 COOH + NH 3
solution: Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid. Decarboxylation refers to the reaction in which carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide to form hydrocarbons when their sodium salt are heated with soda lime. e.g. {tex} \begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{Na^+}} \\ {\text { sodium }} \\ {\text { ehanoate }}\end{array} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{Sodalime{\text{ }}(mixture{\text{ }}of{\text{ }}NaOH\,and\,Ca0\,\,in\,3{\text{ and 1 }}ratio)}}{/tex} {tex} \begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{4}} \\ {\text { Methane }}\end{array} {/tex} + Na 2 CO 3 C 6 H 5 CONH 2 {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{{H_3}{O^ + }}}{/tex} C 6 H 5 COOH + NH 3
solution_html:
1. Cannizzaro reaction Aldehydes which do not have n-H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with cone. alkali this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid.

    2. Decarboxylation refers to the reaction in which carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide to form hydrocarbons when their sodium salt are heated with soda lime. e.g.
      {tex} \begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO} \overline{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{Na^+}} \\ {\text { sodium }} \\ {\text { ehanoate }}\end{array} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{Sodalime{\text{ }}(mixture{\text{ }}of{\text{ }}NaOH\,and\,Ca0\,\,in\,3{\text{ and 1 }}ratio)}}{/tex} {tex} \begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{CH}_{4}} \\ {\text { Methane }}\end{array} {/tex} + Na2CO3


    3. C6H5CONH2 {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{{H_3}{O^ + }}}{/tex} C6H5COOH + NH3

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain the mechanism of nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl group and give one example of such addition reactions.
question_text: Explain the mechanism of nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl group and give one example of such addition reactions.
explanation: Step I: A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom of the polar carbonyl group from a direction approximately perpendicular to the plane of sp 2 hybridised orbitals of carbonyl carbon. The hybridisation of carbon changes from sp 2 to sp 3 in this process and a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate is produced. Step II: This intermediate captures a proton from the reaction medium to give the electrically neutral product. The net result is addition of Nu - and H + across the carbon-oxygen double bond is shown in the figure. An example is given below: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCN \to \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{CN} }^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} }\limits_{Cyanohydrin}{/tex}
solution: Step I: A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom of the polar carbonyl group from a direction approximately perpendicular to the plane of sp 2 hybridised orbitals of carbonyl carbon. The hybridisation of carbon changes from sp 2 to sp 3 in this process and a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate is produced. Step II: This intermediate captures a proton from the reaction medium to give the electrically neutral product. The net result is addition of Nu - and H + across the carbon-oxygen double bond is shown in the figure. An example is given below: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCN \to \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{CN} }^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} }\limits_{Cyanohydrin}{/tex}
solution_html:
Step I: A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom of the polar carbonyl group from a direction approximately perpendicular to the plane of sp2 hybridised orbitals of carbonyl carbon. The hybridisation of carbon changes from sp2 to sp3 in this process and a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate is produced. 

Step II: This intermediate captures a proton from the reaction medium to give the electrically neutral product. The net result is addition of Nu-  and H+ across the carbon-oxygen double bond is shown in the figure.

An example is given below:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} + HCN \to \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{CN} }^{\mathop |\limits^{OH} } - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} }\limits_{Cyanohydrin}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A ketone A(C4H8O) which undergoes a haloform reaction and gives compound B on reduction. B on heating with sulphuric acid gives a compound C which forms mono-ozonide D. D on hydrolysis with zinc dust gives only E. Identify A, B, C, D and E. Write the reactions involved.
question_text: A ketone A(C 4 H 8 O) which undergoes a haloform reaction and gives compound B on reduction. B on heating with sulphuric acid gives a compound C which forms mono-ozonide D. D on hydrolysis with zinc dust gives only E. Identify A, B, C, D and E. Write the reactions involved.
explanation: Since A gives haloform test, it must contain -COCH 3 group. Thus, its possible formula C 2 H 5 COCH 3 . Since A on reduction gives B which on heating with sulphuric acid gives C that forms mono-ozonide (i.e. undergoes ozonolysis), this suggests that C is alkene which is formed by dehydration of alcohol. Hence, B is alcohol. Therefore, C 2 H 5 COCH 3 on reduction gives butan-2-ol (A) which on dehydration gives but-2-ene (C). Ozonolysis of but-2-ene gives only acetaldehyde (E). The reactions involved are: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}} \\ {(A)} \\ {\text { Buan-2-one }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{LiAl{H_4}}]{{[H]}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{(B)} - \mathop {C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(Alcohol)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{conc\,{H_2}S{O_4}/\Delta }}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_{(C)} \mathop {CH - C{H_3}}\limits_{Alkene} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{O_3}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{Zn/ H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {(E)} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} (A) gives iodoform reaction as CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + 3I 2 + 4NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CHI 3 + C 2 H 5 COO - Na + + 3NaI + 3H 2 O
solution: Since A gives haloform test, it must contain -COCH 3 group. Thus, its possible formula C 2 H 5 COCH 3 . Since A on reduction gives B which on heating with sulphuric acid gives C that forms mono-ozonide (i.e. undergoes ozonolysis), this suggests that C is alkene which is formed by dehydration of alcohol. Hence, B is alcohol. Therefore, C 2 H 5 COCH 3 on reduction gives butan-2-ol (A) which on dehydration gives but-2-ene (C). Ozonolysis of but-2-ene gives only acetaldehyde (E). The reactions involved are: {tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}} \\ {(A)} \\ {\text { Buan-2-one }}\end{array}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{LiAl{H_4}}]{{[H]}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{(B)} - \mathop {C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(Alcohol)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{conc\,{H_2}S{O_4}/\Delta }}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_{(C)} \mathop {CH - C{H_3}}\limits_{Alkene} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{O_3}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{Zn/ H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {(E)} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex} (A) gives iodoform reaction as CH 3 COCH 2 CH 3 + 3I 2 + 4NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CHI 3 + C 2 H 5 COO - Na + + 3NaI + 3H 2 O
solution_html:
Since A gives haloform test, it must contain -COCH3 group. Thus, its possible formula C2H5COCH3. Since A on reduction gives B which on heating with sulphuric acid gives C that forms mono-ozonide (i.e. undergoes ozonolysis), this suggests that C is alkene which is formed by dehydration of alcohol. Hence, B is alcohol. Therefore, C2H5COCH3 on reduction gives butan-2-ol (A) which on dehydration gives but-2-ene (C). Ozonolysis of but-2-ene gives only acetaldehyde (E). The reactions involved are:
{tex}\begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}} \\ {(A)} \\ {\text { Buan-2-one }}\end{array}{/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{LiAl{H_4}}]{{[H]}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{(B)} - \mathop {C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(Alcohol)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{{conc\,{H_2}S{O_4}/\Delta }}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - CH\mathop = \limits_{(C)} \mathop {CH - C{H_3}}\limits_{Alkene} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{O_3}{/tex}
 {tex}\xrightarrow{Zn/ H_2O}{/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{c}{2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}} \\ {(E)} \\ {\text { Acetaldehyde }}\end{array}{/tex}
(A) gives iodoform reaction as
CH3COCH2CH3 + 3I2 + 4NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} CHI3 + C2H5COO-Na+ + 3NaI + 3H2O

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Illustrate the following name reactions: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
  2. How are the following conversions carried out?
    1. Ethyl cyanide to ethanoic acid
    2. Butan-1-ol to butanoic acid
    3. Methyl benzene to benzoic acid
question_text: Illustrate the following name reactions: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction How are the following conversions carried out? Ethyl cyanide to ethanoic acid Butan-1-ol to butanoic acid Methyl benzene to benzoic acid
explanation: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) Reaction Carboxylic acids having {tex}\alpha{/tex} -hydrogen atom are halogenated at the a-position on treatment with chlorine or bromine in the presence of small amount of red phosphorus to give {tex}\alpha{/tex} -halocarboxylic acids. The reaction is known as Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction. question_html: An organic compound A on treatment with ethyl alcohol gives a carboxylic acid Band compound C. Hydrolysis of C under acidified conditions gives B and D. Oxidation of D with KMnO4 also gives B. B on heating with Ca(OH)2 gives E having molecular formula C3H6O. E does not give Tollen's test and does not reduce Fehling's solution but forms a 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone. Identify A, B, C, D and E.
question_text: An organic compound A on treatment with ethyl alcohol gives a carboxylic acid Band compound C. Hydrolysis of C under acidified conditions gives B and D. Oxidation of D with KMnO 4 also gives B. B on heating with Ca(OH) 2 gives E having molecular formula C 3 H 6 O. E does not give Tollen's test and does not reduce Fehling's solution but forms a 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone. Identify A, B, C, D and E.
explanation: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{(C)} + {H_2}O\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Dry\,\,distillation}]{{Ca{{(OH)}_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{(E)} + CaC{O_3}{/tex} E does not give Tollen's reagent test and does not reduce Fehling's solution as it is ketone. But it forms a 2,4-dinitophenylhydrazone. A = , B = CH 3 COOH, C = CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 , D = CH 3 CH 2 OH, E = CH 3 COCH 3
solution: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{(C)} + {H_2}O\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Dry\,\,distillation}]{{Ca{{(OH)}_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{(E)} + CaC{O_3}{/tex} E does not give Tollen's reagent test and does not reduce Fehling's solution as it is ketone. But it forms a 2,4-dinitophenylhydrazone. A = , B = CH 3 COOH, C = CH 3 COOC 2 H 5 , D = CH 3 CH 2 OH, E = CH 3 COCH 3
solution_html:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{(C)} + {H_2}O\xrightarrow{{{H^ + }}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)}{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{KMn{O_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COOH}\limits_{(B)}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{Dry\,\,distillation}]{{Ca{{(OH)}_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_{(E)} + CaC{O_3}{/tex}
E does not give Tollen's reagent test and does not reduce Fehling's solution as it is ketone. But it forms a 2,4-dinitophenylhydrazone.
A = , B = CH3COOH, C = CH3COOC2H5, D = CH3CH2OH, E = CH3COCH3

subject: Chemistry
question_html: An organic compound contains 69.77% carbon, 11.63% hydrogen and rest oxygen. The molecular mass of the compound is 86. It does not reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and give positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation it gives ethanoic and propanoic acid. Write the possible structure of the compound.
question_text: An organic compound contains 69.77% carbon, 11.63% hydrogen and rest oxygen. The molecular mass of the compound is 86. It does not reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and give positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation it gives ethanoic and propanoic acid. Write the possible structure of the compound.
explanation: Percentage of carbon = 69.77 %, Percentage of hydrogen = 11.63 %, Percentage of oxygen = {100 - (69.77 + 11.63)}% = 18.6 % Thus, the ratio of the number of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms in the organic compound is given as: {tex}C:H:O = \frac{{69.77}}{{12}}:\frac{{11.63}}{1}:\frac{{18.6}}{{16}}{/tex} =5.81: 11.63: 1.16 =5:10:1 Therefore, the empirical formula of the compound is C 5 H 10 O. Now, the empirical formula mass of the compound can be given as: {tex}5 \times 12 + 10 \times 1 + 1 \times 16{/tex} = 86 Molecular mass of the compound = 86 Therefore, the molecular formula of the compound is given C 5 H 10 O. Since the given compound does not reduce Tollen's reagent, it is not an aldehyde. Again, the compound forms sodium hydrogen sulphate addition products and gives a positive iodoform test. Since the compound is not an aldehyde, it must be a methyl ketone. The given compound also gives a mixture of ethanoic acid and propanoic acid. Hence, the given compound is Pentan-2-one. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{{\text{Pentan - 2 - ol}}} {/tex} The given reactions can be explained by the following equations:

Now, the empirical formula mass of the compound can be given as: {tex}5 \times 12 + 10 \times 1 + 1 \times 16{/tex} = 86 Molecular mass of the compound = 86 Therefore, the molecular formula of the compound is given C 5 H 10 O. Since the given compound does not reduce Tollen's reagent, it is not an aldehyde. Again, the compound forms sodium hydrogen sulphate addition products and gives a positive iodoform test. Since the compound is not an aldehyde, it must be a methyl ketone. The given compound also gives a mixture of ethanoic acid and propanoic acid. Hence, the given compound is Pentan-2-one. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{{\text{Pentan - 2 - ol}}} {/tex} The given reactions can be explained by the following equations:
