question_html: Compound A (C6H12O2)on reduction with LiAlH4 yields two compounds B and C. The compound Bon oxidation gave D which on treatment with aqueous alkali and subsequent heating furnished E. The latter on catalytic hydrogenation gave C. The compound D on further oxidation gave CH3COOH. Deduce the structures of A, B, C, D and E.
question_text: Compound A (C 6 H 12 O 2 )on reduction with LiAlH 4 yields two compounds B and C. The compound Bon oxidation gave D which on treatment with aqueous alkali and subsequent heating furnished E. The latter on catalytic hydrogenation gave C. The compound D on further oxidation gave CH 3 COOH. Deduce the structures of A, B, C, D and E.
explanation: As compound A on reduction with LiAlH 4 yields two compounds, so A must be an ester. D gave CH 3 COOHon oxidation, so it must be CH 3 CHO and it is obtained by the oxidation of B, so B is CH 3 CH 2 OH. Further, D being an aldehyde having {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H, undergoes aldol condensation and gives {tex}\alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} unsaturated aldehyde which gives C on hydrogenation. Thus, compound A is {tex}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } {H_2}{/tex} and the reactions involved are as follows: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(A)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}C{H_3}COOH{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{condensation} }]{{NaOH}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } HC{H_2}CHO{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }C{H_3}CH = \mathop {CHCHO}\limits_{(E)} \xrightarrow[{Pd/C}]{{{H_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)}{/tex}
solution: As compound A on reduction with LiAlH 4 yields two compounds, so A must be an ester. D gave CH 3 COOHon oxidation, so it must be CH 3 CHO and it is obtained by the oxidation of B, so B is CH 3 CH 2 OH. Further, D being an aldehyde having {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H, undergoes aldol condensation and gives {tex}\alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} unsaturated aldehyde which gives C on hydrogenation. Thus, compound A is {tex}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } {H_2}{/tex} and the reactions involved are as follows: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(A)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}C{H_3}COOH{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{condensation} }]{{NaOH}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } HC{H_2}CHO{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }C{H_3}CH = \mathop {CHCHO}\limits_{(E)} \xrightarrow[{Pd/C}]{{{H_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)}{/tex}
solution_html:
As compound A on reduction with LiAlH4 yields two compounds, so A must be an ester. D gave CH3COOHon oxidation, so it must be CH3CHO and it is obtained by the oxidation of B, so B is CH3CH2OH.
Further, D being an aldehyde having {tex}\alpha{/tex}-H, undergoes aldol condensation and gives {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} unsaturated aldehyde which gives C on hydrogenation. Thus, compound A is {tex}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{C{H_3}} } {H_2}{/tex} and the reactions involved are as follows:

  1. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop {||}\limits_O } OC{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{(A)}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}}{/tex}  {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(B)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow{{[O]}}C{H_3}COOH{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{(D)} \xrightarrow[{\mathop {Aldol}\limits_{condensation} }]{{NaOH}}C{H_3}\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } HC{H_2}CHO{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }C{H_3}CH = \mathop {CHCHO}\limits_{(E)} \xrightarrow[{Pd/C}]{{{H_2}}}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{(C)}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: An organic compound A (C3H6O) is resistant to oxidation but forms compound B (C3H8O) on reduction. B reacts with HBr to form the compound C. C with Mg forms Grignard's reagent D which reacts with A to form a product which on hydrolysis gives E. Identify A to E.
question_text: An organic compound A (C 3 H 6 O) is resistant to oxidation but forms compound B (C 3 H 8 O) on reduction. B reacts with HBr to form the compound C. C with Mg forms Grignard's reagent D which reacts with A to form a product which on hydrolysis gives E. Identify A to E.
explanation: The possible structures of the molecular formula C 3 H 6 O are CH 3 COCH 3 (acetone) and CH 3 CH 2 CHO (propanal), the former one is resistant to oxidation. Thus, A is a ketone, i.e. acetone. A on reduction gives B. Thus, B is a 2 o alcohol, i.e. propan-2-ol. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_A {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{LiAlH_4}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{Propan-2-ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} Propan-2-ol reacts with HBr to give 2-bromopropane. {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH_3\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}\,}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} C (2-bromopropane) gives D (Grignard's reagent) with Mg {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H}\limits_{MgBr} }\limits_{(D)} - C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} D reacts with acetone A to give an adduct which on hydrolysis gives a 3 o alcohol E.
solution: The possible structures of the molecular formula C 3 H 6 O are CH 3 COCH 3 (acetone) and CH 3 CH 2 CHO (propanal), the former one is resistant to oxidation. Thus, A is a ketone, i.e. acetone. A on reduction gives B. Thus, B is a 2 o alcohol, i.e. propan-2-ol. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_A {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{LiAlH_4}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{Propan-2-ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} Propan-2-ol reacts with HBr to give 2-bromopropane. {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH_3\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}\,}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} C (2-bromopropane) gives D (Grignard's reagent) with Mg {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H}\limits_{MgBr} }\limits_{(D)} - C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} D reacts with acetone A to give an adduct which on hydrolysis gives a 3 o alcohol E.
solution_html:
The possible structures of the molecular formula C3H6O are CH3COCH3 (acetone) and CH3CH2CHO (propanal), the former one is resistant to oxidation. Thus, A is a ketone, i.e. acetone.

  1. A on reduction gives B. Thus, B is a 2o alcohol, i.e. propan-2-ol.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COC{H_3}}\limits_A {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{LiAlH_4}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{Propan-2-ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex}    {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex}
  2. Propan-2-ol reacts with HBr to give 2-bromopropane.
    {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} C{H_3}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH_3\,\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} \,C{H_3}}\limits_{\Pr opan - 2 - ol} }\limits_{(B)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{HBr}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}\,}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex}
  3. C (2-bromopropane) gives D (Grignard's reagent) with Mg
    {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{OH} }\limits_{\ 2-bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} C{H_3}{/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop { - C}\limits_| H}\limits_{MgBr} }\limits_{(D)} - C{H_3}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathop {\mathop {CH\,\mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{OH} } H\,C{H_3}}\limits_{2 - bromopropane} }\limits_{(C)} {/tex} + Mg {tex}\xrightarrow{Ether}{/tex} 
  4. D reacts with acetone A to give an adduct which on hydrolysis gives a 3o alcohol E.

question_text: Complete the following reaction by giving the missing starting material, reagent or product as required: Describe the following reactions: Cannizzaro reaction Cross aldol condensation
explanation: B 2 H 6 /THF polarised by H 2 O 2 /NaOH and PCC. Cannizzaro reaction: Aldehydes which do not have {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H atoms undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on treatment with conc. alkali, this reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction. In this reaction, one molecule of aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another molecule is oxidised to the salt of carboxylic acid. Cross aldol condensation: When aldol condensation is carried out between two different aldehydes or ketones or between an aldehyde or a ketone and at least one of them contain {tex}\alpha{/tex} -H, it is called cross aldol condensation. If both of them contain a-hydrogen atoms, it gives a mixture of four products. subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. How would you account for the following?
    1. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones toward nucleophiles.
    2. The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones are lower than that of the corresponding acids.
    3. The aldehydes and ketones undergo a number of addition reactions.
  2. Give chemical tests to distinguish between
    1. acetaldehyde and benzaldehyde.
    2. propanone and propanol
question_text: How would you account for the following? Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones toward nucleophiles. The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones are lower than that of the corresponding acids. The aldehydes and ketones undergo a number of addition reactions. Give chemical tests to distinguish between acetaldehyde and benzaldehyde. propanone and propanol
explanation: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones toward nucleophiles due to steric and electronic reasons. Sterically, the presence of two relatively large substituents in ketones hinders the approach of nucleophile to carbonyl carbon than in aldehyde having only one such substituent. This is because of the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids as they have polar-OH group due to which carboxylic acids exist as associated molecules and hence, high temperature is required to break H-bond, which leads to their high boiling points. Due to the present of polar carbonyl group the carbonyl carbon bears partial positive charge, and thus undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. Due to the presence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group in acetaldehyde it undergoes iodoform reaction by giving yellow ppt. with NaOH/I 2 , while benzaldehyde does not show this (iodoform) test due to the absence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group. {tex}C{H_3} - C - H + 2NaOI \to HCOONa + \mathop {CH{I_3} \downarrow }\limits_{Yellow\,\,ppt} + 2NaOH{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt. of CHI 3 Propanone gives iodoform test with NaOHI/I 2 and gives yellow ppt. of CHI 3 while propanol does not give this test.
solution: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones toward nucleophiles due to steric and electronic reasons. Sterically, the presence of two relatively large substituents in ketones hinders the approach of nucleophile to carbonyl carbon than in aldehyde having only one such substituent. This is because of the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids as they have polar-OH group due to which carboxylic acids exist as associated molecules and hence, high temperature is required to break H-bond, which leads to their high boiling points. Due to the present of polar carbonyl group the carbonyl carbon bears partial positive charge, and thus undergo nucleophilic addition reaction. Due to the presence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group in acetaldehyde it undergoes iodoform reaction by giving yellow ppt. with NaOH/I 2 , while benzaldehyde does not show this (iodoform) test due to the absence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group. {tex}C{H_3} - C - H + 2NaOI \to HCOONa + \mathop {CH{I_3} \downarrow }\limits_{Yellow\,\,ppt} + 2NaOH{/tex} {tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt. of CHI 3 Propanone gives iodoform test with NaOHI/I 2 and gives yellow ppt. of CHI 3 while propanol does not give this test.
solution_html:
1. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones toward nucleophiles due to steric and electronic reasons. Sterically, the presence of two relatively large substituents in ketones hinders the approach of nucleophile to carbonyl carbon than in aldehyde having only one such substituent. 
    2. This is because of the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in carboxylic acids as they have polar-OH group due to which carboxylic acids exist as associated molecules and hence, high temperature is required to break H-bond, which leads to their high boiling points.
    3. Due to the present of polar carbonyl group   the carbonyl carbon bears partial positive charge, and thus undergo nucleophilic addition reaction.
    1. Due to the presence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group in acetaldehyde it undergoes iodoform reaction by giving yellow ppt. with NaOH/I2, while benzaldehyde does not show this (iodoform) test due to the absence of {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - {/tex} group.
      {tex}C{H_3} - C - H + 2NaOI \to HCOONa + \mathop {CH{I_3} \downarrow }\limits_{Yellow\,\,ppt} + 2NaOH{/tex}
      {tex}{C_6}{H_5} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop {||}\limits^O } - H\xrightarrow{{NaOI}}{/tex} No yellow ppt. of CHI3 
    2. Propanone gives iodoform test with NaOHI/I2 and gives yellow ppt. of CHI3 while propanol does not give this test.

question_text: An organic compound A (C 7 H 6 Cl 2 )on treatment with NaOH solution gives another compound B(C 7 H 6 O). B on oxidation gives an acid C (C 7 H 6 O2) which on treatment with a mixture of cone. HNO 3 and H 2 SO 4 gives a compound D (C 7 H 5 NO 4 ). B on treatment with cone. NaOH gives a compound E (C 7 H 8 O) and C 6 H 5 COONa. Deduce the structures of A, B, C, D and E.
explanation: Since, compound A on treatment with NaOH gives C 7 H 6 O,i.e. only one O-atom is introduced, both the Cl-atoms must be present on the same carbon atom. The molecular formula C 7 H 6 O suggest that degree of unsaturation is (5) so, the structure of B is C 6 H 5 CHO. Thus, A must be C 6 H 5 CHCl 2 . The relevant equations are as:

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Nucleophilic addition will be most favoured in:
question_text: Nucleophilic addition will be most favoured in:

options.explanation: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones towards nucleophiles. The addition of nucleophiles is based upon the positive charge present on carbon atom of group. in aldehyde group is present with at least one alkyl group (except formaldehyde) which has +1 effect and which decreases the positive charge of carbon, thereby making the attack to nucleophilic in ketones having minimum of two alkyl groups. Thus nucelophilic addition order is: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{({\mathrm{CH}}_3)}_2\mathrm{CO}\;>{/tex} {tex}\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}

explanation: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones towards nucleophiles. Thus nucelophilic addition order is: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{({\mathrm{CH}}_3)}_2\mathrm{CO}\;>{/tex} {tex}\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}
